text: Even nature's most unpleasant creatures have important secrets, but who would want a swarm of cockroaches coming towards them? Yet one of the greatest differences between natural and human technologies relates to robustness. Robust systems are stable in complex and new environments. Remarkably, cockroaches can self-stabilize when running over rough terrain. When equipped with a jet pack or subjected to a perturbation like an earthquake, their precisely tuned legs allow them to self-stabilize without using any brainpower. They can move over complex terrain like grass with ease, without becoming destabilized. We discovered a new behavior where, due to their shape, they automatically roll to their side to navigate through an artificial test section of grass. Robust systems can perform multiple tasks with the same structure. Here's a new behavior we've discovered.

Indeed, for 20 years, I worked in finance. I knew everything about finance, for sure. I knew the global economy because it was my life. All the topics that you could bring up about ecology, the end of the world, etc., that I knew like the back of my hand—I didn't really believe in them. In 2007, an extremely unusual event occurred; you may remember, the subprime crisis, the collapse of big American banks. It was an electric shock. I had a fair few stormy conversations with my wife about the matter, about my job, on the limits of the model. Besides the crisis, I realized that I was completely wrong, that we were heading for disaster.

Wales' newly appointed Health Minister, Dr. Brian Gibbons, has acknowledged that the country can benefit from England's strategies in addressing its lengthy waiting lists. On his first full day in office, following the dismissal of his predecessor, Jane Hutt, Dr. Gibbons conceded that significant challenges lie ahead, but expressed confidence that the fundamental framework is in place to tackle these issues. However, opposition parties, including Plaid Cymru and the Liberal Democrats, have called for a policy overhaul under Dr. Gibbons' leadership. In response to her dismissal, Ms. Hutt defended her tenure, emphasizing that waiting times and lists comprise only a fraction of the broader healthcare agenda. Dr. Gibbons, a former GP in the Upper Afan Valley, emphasized the need for a period of consolidation within the NHS, following a period of substantial change and reform. The issue of waiting lists had been a persistent challenge during Ms. Hutt's five-and-a-half-year tenure as Wales' first Health Minister.

What can I say... *Harry Potter and the Prisoner of Azkaban* was bloody brilliant!! I went to see it right after school on Friday. Yes, that's right—I saw it on opening day!! (Thanks, Mike, for the ticket.) Daniel Radcliffe, aka Harry Potter, has grown up so fast, and he is sooo cute!! Malfoy too, haha. Okay, well... The movie was overall great—I give it 4½ stars because they left out some important details!! Still, it was really good... I didn’t dress up for the movie, but I was wearing a Ravenclaw tie, even though I like Gryffindor better. I might go see it again in about two weeks.

It's 11:14pm MST, and I'm sitting in my bedroom fixing the personal computer of the personnel director from my job. Not that I mind doing it, but I sometimes wonder how often I find myself fixing someone else's computer as a favour. I'm glad to be able to show love and care for people in this way, but where is the line drawn before it becomes a case of being taken advantage of? The last couple of weeks, I've been staying up late reading and such, so I was looking forward to an early night, until I realized it would be more prudent to return this computer to its owner. *laugh* No rest for the weary, right? So I ended up watching Joe Schmo 2 again tonight.

Cardinal Cormac Murphy-O'Connor, head of the Catholic Church in England and Wales, has strongly condemned the Iraq War as a catastrophic misuse of resources that could have alleviated global poverty. In a pointed Christmas message delivered during his Midnight Mass and reiterated on BBC Radio 4's *Thought for the Day*, the Cardinal lamented how billions of pounds spent on Middle Eastern conflicts had been diverted from urgent humanitarian needs.  

Joining the Archbishop of Canterbury in voicing opposition to the war, Cardinal Murphy-O'Connor described the military expenditure as "a terrible waste" while millions worldwide suffer from extreme poverty, malnutrition, and preventable diseases. He called for 2005 to become a turning point in the fight against global inequality, urging concerted efforts to "make poverty history" through targeted campaigns and moral leadership.

What defines a parent? This question is not easily answered, especially in today's world of adoption, stepfamilies, and surrogate mothers. Many parents navigate challenging questions and significant decisions. For instance, should we inform our child about their conception through sperm donation? If so, when is the right time, and what terminology should we use? While sperm donors are often labeled as "biological fathers," is it appropriate to call them "fathers"? As a philosopher and social scientist, I have extensively explored these complex issues regarding the concept of parenthood. Today, however, I will share insights gained from conversations with parents and children. They understand what truly matters in a family, even when their family structure may differ from the norm.

Doves soar to UK album summit. Manchester rock band Doves have entered the UK album chart at number one with their new release, Some Cities. The trio replace flamboyant US act Scissor Sisters at the top. The album follows single Black and White Town, which reached number six. R&B star Nelly has the new number one single with Over and Over, which sees him team up with Tim McGraw. Girls Aloud, Akon, and Kaiser Chiefs all have new singles in the top ten, as do The Futureheads and Usher. The latest Elvis Presley re-release, (Marie's the Name) His Latest Flame, entered the chart at number three, one place ahead of Girls Aloud's Wake Me Up. Hip-hop performer Akon's Locked Up is at number five, while hotly-tipped Leeds band Kaiser Chiefs have their second chart hit at number six with Oh My God.

Without warning, an overwhelming fatigue has consumed me for the past two days—a deep, bone-weary exhaustion. Yesterday evening, I returned home and collapsed into sleep, only rousing when my mother called me at half past eight. Upon waking in the pitch-black room, disoriented and struggling to grasp reality, her voice was the only anchor in those fleeting moments of confusion. I can't fathom why I feel so utterly drained...  

The morning brought pleasant weather—a light drizzle as I arrived. Meanwhile, I hear Calcutta has endured three straight days of rain.

Alright. Let me explain a few things, as a person very interested in politics, I was very interested in this game when I first heard about it. I thought, 'oh wow! A simulator of the road to becoming president! That sounds fun!' Well, it really wasn't. The game starts you off and says you need to pick a side: Republican or Democrat, there is no Independent, or third party. A little disappointed, but still optimistic, I carried on to see what I could do. I will give the game credit, the character customization, although limited, is pretty fun to mess around with. It's the only time it's an option to wear your head or not in a campaign. Literally.

Warframe is a fun game for the initial hours of play. As you progress beyond those few enjoyable hours, you'll notice that the game's offerings begin to dwindle, with repetition dominating its combat, enemies, and gameplay. The redeeming quality is that the developers are actively working to deliver regular updates, which often bring significant changes to the game's systems, new Warframes, and other enhancements. It's an excellent choice for casual play. While I'm skeptical about its long-term appeal, I recommend giving it a try. If you're seeking a quick fix, Warframe is worth a try. Since it's free, there's no harm in giving it a shot.

Let's catch up when you get back to Houston. We can go to lunch or set up a time to meet in the office. Mark, I'm attaching the March report for EGA. I understand the length may be longer than you'd like, but we'll continue to work on condensing it. I left some discussion on troubled projects like SECLP and DABHOL. I hope everything is well. I've been on the road for some time and am currently in Singapore. I'll likely head back to Bombay for at least the first few days of next week. Best, Mark.

You can see by the hours I put into this game that I enjoy it. This is everything I wished a search game from COD would be. But don't buy this game thinking it's like COD, Battlefield, or any other shooter game. It's a game in its own class and should be treated as such. Can't tell you how many times I hear new players ♥♥♥♥♥ and complain about getting killed really fast. Oh, God forbid you get shot in the face and ♥♥♥♥ing die. It's not like that doesn't happen in real life or anything. Edit: Ok, so I've been playing this game for a while now. Thrown all kinds of money at it. One of the best MP games around. A good thing to play when you're not feeling CS:GO.

Disappointed Scott in Solid Start Allan Scott is confident of winning a medal at next week's European Indoor Championships after a solid debut on the international circuit. The 22-year-old Scot finished fourth in the 60m hurdles at the Jose M. Cagigal Memorial meeting in Madrid. "It was definitely a learning curve and I certainly haven't ruled out challenging for a medal next week," said the East Kilbride athlete. The race was won by Felipe Vivancos, who equaled the Spanish record. Sweden's Robert Kronberg was second, with Haiti's Dudley Dorival in third. Scott was slightly disappointed with his run in the final. He won his heat in 7.64secs but ran 0.04secs slower in his first IAAF Indoor Grand Prix circuit final.

Yukos, heading back to US courts, Russian oil and gas company Yukos is due in a US court on Thursday as it continues to fight for its survival. The firm is in the process of being broken up by Russian authorities in order to pay a $27 billion (£14 billion) tax bill. Yukos has filed for bankruptcy in the US, hoping to use international business law to halt the forced sale of its key oil production unit, Yugasnkneftegas. The unit was, however, sold for $9.4 billion to state oil firm Rosneft, but only after the state auction had been disrupted. Yukos lawyers now say the auction violated US bankruptcy law. The company and its main shareholders have vowed to go after any company that buys its assets, using all and every legal means.

This is, without a doubt, my all-time favorite first-person shooter. It's not for those who prefer a run-and-gun Rambo style. The recoil and ballistics are depicted with realism, as even a single hit to the chest, neck, or head with a rifle-caliber bullet is almost always lethal, unless it's just a graze. Bullets drop over distances, necessitating short, controlled bursts to stay on target. There's no limitless ammunition; if you frequently reload halfway through your magazine, you'll end up with a pouch filled with partially full magazines. You can't sprint indefinitely; prolonged running will affect your aim as you struggle to catch your breath. To stabilize your aim, rest your weapon on your elbows when prone or on a window sill. Standing results in compromised accuracy.

We're seen as the organization that serves as a last resort for failed social policy. I have no control over who comes to us or how long they stay. We receive individuals for whom all other options have been exhausted, people who have fallen through the cracks of every other social safety net. Since they can't be contained elsewhere, we must. Our role is to contain and control them. Over the years, as a prison system, as a nation, and as a society, we've become very skilled at this, but that's not something to be proud of. Today, we incarcerate more people per capita than any other country in the world. We currently have more black men in prison than were enslaved in 1850.

It's 11 14 pm MST, and I'm currently sitting in my bedroom, fixing the personal computer of my job's personnel director. While I don't mind doing it, I often find myself wondering when the line is crossed from being helpful to being taken advantage of. I'm grateful to be able to use my skills to assist others, but I've been staying up late reading lately, so I was looking forward to an early night, until I realized it would be more practical to get the computer back to its owner. No rest for the weary, indeed! As I finished the task, I found myself watching Joe Schmo 2 again tonight.

Dear Martin,

I hope this email finds you well. I wanted to follow up on my previous message regarding the summer internship and e-procurement issues. I did not find anyone at EBS who seemed to have knowledge or involvement in this area. However, I did discover that Enron Corp has an initiative called iBuyIt, which is a system being deployed for e-procurement across the corporation and Enron North America. It's expected to be rolled out to EBS sometime later this year. John Gillespie is leading the iBuyIt initiative, and I believe he would be the best point of contact. I left a voice mail for him, but I haven't received a response yet. I would greatly appreciate it if you could reach out to him on my behalf.

Best regards,
Martin Vince J. Kaminski

Yesterday, my colleagues from the bank and I embarked on an excursion to the picturesque Guintubdan, La Carolota. For most of us, it was a maiden visit, and we were eager to explore its natural wonders. Our journey began with a 30-minute trek to the falls, a moderately challenging hike that left us all breathless. However, the breathtaking scenery that awaited us made every step worthwhile. As we immersed ourselves in the icy waters, our bodies began to numb from the cold, but the sensation was invigorating. Due to time constraints, we limited our stay to under an hour, opting to proceed to the nearby waterfalls instead. Another 30-minute trek ensued, but the reward was well worth the effort. The falls exuded a serene tranquility, and the surrounding landscape was nothing short of breathtaking. As I stood there, surrounded by nature's splendor, I felt an overwhelming sense of unity with the earth. The experience was almost meditative, with the cold air and water lulling me into a state of deep relaxation, as if I were communing with the very essence of the natural world.

Undertale comes together to create something greater than the sum of its parts. The graphics are solid for a retro title and the music is pleasant, but together they create some truly beautiful and impactful moments. The script ranges from comedic to dramatic to heartbreaking to hopeful, sometimes all at once, and it does this while cleverly subverting traditional RPG tropes. And all of this is achieved without even delving into the world, characters, story, or gameplay—subjects I could write entire pages about. But instead, I'll just say this: if you like RPGs, play this game. If you enjoy video games, play this game. If you have seven free hours between now and the end of your life, play this game.

Jamieson Issues Warning to Bigots Scotland's justice minister has warned bigoted soccer fans that she intends to hit them "where it hurts most" by banning them from matches. Cathy Jamieson said exclusion orders are among a series of measures being considered in the Scottish Executive's campaign against sectarianism. She praised Celtic and Rangers for their efforts in addressing the problem. However, the minister stated that stopping sectarian abuse associated with Old Firm matches is a key objective. Ms. Jamieson was speaking ahead of the third-round Scottish Cup clash between the Glasgow clubs at Parkhead on Sunday. The sectarianism long associated with certain sections of the fanbase from both clubs has become a significant target for the Executive. Last week, Ms. Jamieson and First Minister Jack McConnell met with supporters' representatives from both clubs to discuss the issue.

This game is an absolute great strategy game! Having played every single other war game (European Escalation and Albion), I have to say this one fits well. The number of units is great. Not sure exactly how many units there are, but there's a vast amount. The graphics are just beautiful, the sounds are great, and the gameplay overall is epic. The in-game chat box at the main menu is very entertaining, I'm not kidding. However, if you think this game is 'easy', or if you're new to the strategy genre, it could take you some time to get it down. Don't get discouraged, you'll lose a lot of units. Just make sure you plan ahead, always have anti-air, and anti-tank.

So I downloaded the game and played it for a little while, and it was nothing special. I spent more time trying to log in though than actually playing the game. There is a bug where it says that there was an update to the terms of service or whatever, and whenever you clicked 'accept', it would just pop up again. You have to restart the client for it to work. Now that isn't that bad, but why do I need to link my Steam and Gface accounts? They say it is for DLC, but why is that even necessary? It is also not reversible, so now I know that a company I don't care about has access to my Steam information. Now the game itself is so-so. It is easily recognizable as a CoD clone, except somehow they made the gunplay worse... How is that even possible?

A comprehensive difference-in-differences analysis with a carefully controlled control group that differs significantly from a treated group is susceptible to bias from historical events that have distinct effects on the groups. To mitigate this issue, we construct a more closely matched control group by matching a subset of the overall control group to the treated group, which may result in less bias. We investigate this phenomenon in simulation studies. We examine the effect of mountaintop removal mining (MRM) on mortality using a difference-in-differences analysis that leverages the increase in MRM following the 1990 Clean Air Act amendments. For a difference-in-differences analysis of the effect of MRM on mortality, we constructed a more closely matched control group and found a 95% confidence interval that contains substantial adverse effects, as well as small beneficial effects.

Let me recount the most embarrassing incident in my career as a palliative care physician. This took place a few years ago. I was called in as a consultant to assess a woman in her 70s, a retired English professor suffering from pancreatic cancer. My role was to address her issues with pain, nausea, and vomiting. During our consultation, she inquired whether I believed medical marijuana could alleviate her symptoms. Reflecting on my medical school education regarding medical marijuana, I realized, to my chagrin, that I had learned absolutely nothing about it.

Low-symmetry 2D materials, including resorbed and reshifted monolayers, black phosphorus, group-IV monochalcogenides, and borophene, exhibit more intricate atomic arrangements than the hexagonal lattices of graphene, hexagonal boron nitride, and transition metal dichalcogenides. The reduced symmetries of these emerging materials lead to a diverse range of electronic, optical, valley, and spin responses, including ferroelasticity, ferroelectricity, magnetism, spin-wave phenomena, large nonlinear optical properties, photogalvanic effects, and superconductivity. Additionally, these materials can exhibit novel electronic topological properties, nonlinear elastic properties, and structural phase transformations due to their low symmetry. The "Beyond Graphene: Low-Symmetry and Anisotropic 2D Materials" special topic brings together recent experimental and theoretical research on these innovative materials.

Remember Age of Mythology? It's been that long since an RTS game of such quality has been released. Divinity: Dragon Commander comes awfully close to surpassing my personal favorite RTS game. Combining this with a massive Risk-like grand strategy mode is the icing on the cake. I'm trying not to be overly biased, but the fact that this game allows you to transform into a dragon, supporting and commanding your troops while attacking and directing unit production and spawning, is a major draw. I've grown fond of complex RTS games like Civilization V, but those games often try to cram too many mechanics, charts, and resources into a single, convoluted game mode.

Spirit Awards hail *Sideways*—the comedy *Sideways* has dominated this year's Independent Spirit Awards, winning all six awards for which it was nominated. It was named Best Film, while Alexander Payne won Best Director and Best Screenplay, along with writing partner Jim Taylor. The film also won acting awards for stars Paul Giamatti, Thomas Haden Church, and Virginia Madsen. *Sideways* is tipped to do well at Sunday's Oscars, with five nominations. The awards, now in their 20th year, are given to films made outside the traditional studio system and are traditionally held the day before the Oscars. Other winners included Catalina Sandino Moreno, who took Best Actress for her role as a drug smuggler in the Colombian drama *Maria Full of Grace*. Moreno is also nominated for Best Actress at the Oscars. The Best First Screenplay award went to Joshua Marston for *Maria Full of Grace*.

Currently, the majority of refugees reside in cities instead of refugee camps, accounting for over 60 percent of the global refugee population. This urban dwelling highlights the urgent need for a paradigm shift and innovative thinking. Instead of investing in building walls, it would be more beneficial to fund programs that empower refugees to support themselves. (applause) Refugees are often forced to leave behind their possessions, but they retain their skills and knowledge. When given the opportunity, they can lead productive lives, aiding not only themselves but also contributing positively to their host countries' development. I was born in Bukavu, South Kivu, in the Democratic Republic of Congo, as the fifth child in a family of 12.

Well, that's quite an obvious statement up there. I started with that sentence about 12 years ago, initially in the context of developing countries. Yet, you're here from every corner of the world. If you imagine a map of your country, you'll likely realize that in every nation, you could draw small circles marking places where good teachers won't venture. These are also the areas from which trouble often emerges. This presents an ironic issue—good teachers tend to avoid exactly the places where they're most needed. In 1999, I began to address this problem with a straightforward experiment in New Delhi.

[Citizens of the world][we face a global crisis of unprecedented scale][please stand by for a message from...][the Secretary-General of the United Nations, António Guterres] The climate emergency is the defining crisis of our time. We are in a race against time, and we are losing. There is a growing tide of impatience, especially among young people, with global inaction. We need more ambition from all: governments, cities, businesses, investors, and people everywhere. So, I'm pleased you are launching TED Countdown. Your influence and ideas can help accelerate momentum for a carbon-neutral world by 2050. That is the only way to avert the worst impacts of global heating. We have the tools, the science, and the resources. Let us now enter this race with political will and energy.

I encourage everyone to be thoughtful before taking action. In fact, I wish that every person in the world would take a moment to reflect before even getting out of bed in the morning, and then commit to maintaining that level of thoughtfulness throughout the day. If someone isn't ready to do so, perhaps they should consider staying in bed and opting for a restful sleep instead. I'm genuinely frustrated with careless behavior. While I don't claim to be the smartest person, my concerns aren't about intellectual challenges but rather common sense. Therefore, I think it's reasonable to hold people accountable for thoughtless actions. Let's see if I can limit this particular discussion to a few specific groups and save others for another time. Sound good? Great.

Music for the day: 94.7 fm. I almost didn't make it to work on time due to a huge accident at 14th and Constitution that caused traffic chaos in Virginia. Thankfully, I skillfully managed to move my car from the far left lane to the far right and got onto Route 50. This probably means nothing to anyone unless you live around here, so never mind. I'm just happy to be sitting here instead of still stuck in my car. Yesterday, Sara decided to Google me, and it turned out that the email I wrote to Ivan Meisel on ESPN.com was published online. I had no idea; I didn't think it was the most well-written argument, but I guess someone thought otherwise.

New research suggests that cutting the drink-drive limit from 80mg of alcohol per 100ml of blood to 50mg could save more lives than previously thought. A report from University College London claims that this change could save 65 lives per year. The findings are being published by the parliamentary advisory council for transport safety as MPS are due to vote on the government's road safety bill. The bill includes changes to speeding fines but not the drink-drive limit. According to transport expert Professor Richard Allsop, cutting the limit would also lead to 230 fewer serious injuries on the UK's roads compared to 2003. He estimates that this would save the country £119m per year. A previous estimate, contained in a 1998 Department of Transport document, suggested that such a move could save 50 lives per year.

Virgin Radio offers 3G broadcast. UK broadcaster Virgin Radio says it will become the first station in the world to offer radio via 3G mobiles. The radio station, in partnership with technology firm Sydus, will broadcast on selected 2G and high-speed 3G networks. Later this year, listeners will be able to download software from the Virgin website which enables the service. James Cridland, head of new media at Virgin Radio, said: 'It places radio at the heart of the 3G revolution.' Virgin Radio will be the first station made available, followed by two digital stations, Virgin Radio Classic Rock and Virgin Radio Groove. Mr Cridland said: 'This application will enable anyone, anywhere to listen to Virgin Radio simply with the phone in their pocket.'

This paper shows that the eccentric debris rings observed around stars Fomalhaut and HD 202628 are narrower than expected in the standard eccentric planet perturbation scenario, also known as the "pericenter glow." In this scenario, an initially circular and narrow belt of planetesimals at semi-major axis $a$ becomes eccentric due to secular perturbations from an eccentric planet, resulting in a belt of width $2ae_f$. However, in a minor modification of this scenario, narrower belts can arise if the planetesimals are initially eccentric, which could result from earlier planet perturbations during the gas-rich protoplanetary disc phase. Alternatively, a primordial eccentricity could be caused by disc instabilities that increase the disc eccentricity without the need for any planets.

Leeds v Saracens (Fri) at Headingley, Friday, 25 February 2000 GMT. The Tykes have bolstered their squad with the signings of Newcastle prop Ed Kalman and Tom McGee from the Borders on loan, while fly-half Craig McMullen has joined from Narbonne. Meanwhile, Saracens have made four changes, including the selection of Raphael Ibanez at hooker. Simon Raiwalui and Ben Russell are also included in the pack, with Kevin Sorrell coming in at outside centre. Following an early pitch inspection on Friday, the game at Headingley has been given the green light. The Leeds team is as follows: Balshaw; Rees, Christophers, Bell, Doherty; McMullen, Dickens; McGee, Rawlinson, Gerber; Murphy, Palmer (capt), Morgan, Parks, Popham. Replacements: Kalman, Regan, Hyde, Rigney, McMillan, Rock, Vickerman.

We introduce a novel mixed-variable symplectic (MVS) integrator designed for planetary systems, capable of accurately resolving close encounters. This method employs a time regularization technique that preserves the stability advantages of symplectic integrators while dynamically adjusting the step size during planetary close approaches. By implementing a high-order MVS scheme, the algorithm maintains computational efficiency with large time steps in regions of weak perturbations, such as in typical solar system configurations.  

Our results demonstrate the integrator's exceptional precision, successfully handling near-collisions (with separations as small as a fraction of a planetary radius) without reducing the time step below that required for weakly perturbed systems. We validate its long-term stability by simulating a chaotic six-super-Earth system over 50,000 years, observing sustained strong scattering interactions.  

Comparative analysis against hybrid methods, such as Mercury, reveals superior energy conservation at equivalent computational cost, establishing our approach as a robust and efficient solution for high-fidelity planetary dynamics simulations.

I can't think of anyone who single-mindedly pursues one interest in life, and that's all they want to do for the rest of their life. Only around 15% of American workers have traditional full-time jobs. They are half-time, part-time, contract workers, or temps. The term "side hustle" seems to capture the spirit of this era where people are combining multiple pursuits to make a living. The word "side hustle" has its roots in popular African American newspapers of the 1920s, where it referred to some kind of scam. By the 1950s, it was used to describe legitimate work as well. A side hustle differs from a second job in that it is not necessarily motivated by necessity.

Currently, most refugees live in cities rather than in refugee camps. We represent over 60 percent of the global number of refugees. With the majority of refugees living in urban areas, there is a strong need for a paradigm shift and new thinking. Rather than wasting money on building walls, it would be better to spend on programs to help refugees help themselves. (applause) We always have to leave behind all our possessions, but not our skills and knowledge. If allowed to live a productive life, refugees can help themselves and contribute to the development of their host country. I was born in the city of Bukavu, South Kivu, in the Democratic Republic of Congo. I am the fifth-born in a family of 12 children.

"Yukos will return to a US court on Wednesday to seek sanctions against Baikal Finance Group, the little-known firm that has bought its main asset. Yukos will sue Baikal and others involved in the sale of Yuganskneftegas for $20 billion in damages. Yukos' US lawyers will attempt to have Baikal's assets frozen after the Russian government ignored a US court order last week blocking the sale. Baikal's background and its motives for buying the Yuganskneftegas production unit are still unclear. Russian newspapers have claimed that Baikal, which bought the Yuganskneftegas production unit for $9.4 billion (261 billion roubles, £4.8 billion) on Sunday at a state-provoked auction - has strong links with Surgutneftegas, Russia's fourth-biggest oil producer.

Causal inference from observational data is a key objective in many data analyses within the health and social sciences. Yet, academic statistics has often been critical of data analyses aimed at establishing causality. The emergence of the term "data science" offers a historic chance to redefine data analysis in a way that naturally incorporates causal inference from observational data. As others have done before, we categorize the scientific contributions of data science into three types of tasks: description, prediction, and counterfactual prediction (which includes causal inference). Clearly classifying these data science tasks is essential for discussing the data, assumptions, and analytical methods required to effectively achieve each task. We contend that the failure to clearly outline the role of subject-matter expertise in data analysis leads to widespread misunderstandings about data science.

Ordinary differential equation (ODE) is a mathematical model used in many application areas such as climatology, bioinformatics, and chemical engineering with its intuitive appeal to modeling. Despite ODE's wide usage in modeling, the frequent absence of analytic solutions makes it difficult to estimate ODE parameters from the data, especially when the model has many variables and parameters. This paper proposes a Bayesian ODE parameter estimation algorithm that is fast and accurate even for models with many parameters. The proposed method approximates an ODE model with a state-space model based on equations of a numeric solver. It allows fast estimation by avoiding computations of a whole numerical solution in the likelihood. The posterior is obtained by a variational Bayes method, more specifically, the approximate Riemannian conjugate gradient method (Honkela et al.).

Following the suggestion from the Monte Carlo experiments in Jiménez, J. of Turbul. (2020), which indicate that dipoles are as crucial to the dynamics of decaying two-dimensional turbulence as individual vortex cores, it is observed that the kinetic energy of this flow is carried by elongated streams formed by the concatenation of dipoles. Vortices separate into a family of small, fast-moving cores and another family of larger, slowly moving ones, which can be described as 'frozen' into a slowly evolving 'crystal'. The kinematics of both families are very different, and only the former is self-similar. The latter accounts for most of the kinetic energy of the flow, and its vortices form the dipoles and the streams. Mechanisms are discussed for the growth of this slow component.

The tails of diboson production at the LHC are sensitive to the interference between standard model and higher-dimensional operators, which parameterize the effects of heavy new physics. However, helicity selection rules for the diboson scattering amplitudes impose an obstruction to the naïve interference contributions of dimension six operators, resulting in the total diboson rate correction's leading contribution cancelling. In this case, measuring the azimuthal decay angles "resurrects" the interference, recouping sensitivity to the "non-interfering" operators. We explore these signatures in detail, and find that the EFT uncertainties associated with higher-dimensional operators are uniquely well-suppressed by the construction of an asymmetry variable that is only generated by these non-interfering operators, relegating the effects of higher-dimensional, interfering operators to the same status as statistical errors in this observable.

The tails of diboson production at the LHC are sensitive to the interference between the Standard Model and higher-dimension operators parameterizing the effects of heavy new physics. However, helicity selection rules for diboson scattering amplitudes set an obstruction to the naïve interference contributions of dimension-six operators, causing the leading contribution to the total diboson rate correction to cancel. In this case, carefully measuring the azimuthal decay angles "resurrects" the interference, restoring sensitivity to the "non-interfering" operators. We explore these signatures in detail and find that the EFT uncertainties associated with higher-dimensional operators are uniquely well-suppressed by constructing an asymmetry variable generated exclusively by these non-interfering operators, relegating the effects of higher-dimensional, interfering operators to the same status as statistical errors in this observable.

UK debut for Kevin Spacey movie: Hollywood stars Kevin Spacey and Kate Bosworth attended the British premiere of the new film, Beyond the Sea, in London's Leicester Square on Thursday. Spacey, 45, wrote, directed, and starred in the film, inspired by the life of 1950s crooner Bobby Darin. "This is my tribute to someone I think was a remarkable talent," said Spacey, who, as Darin, sings all 18 songs on the film's soundtrack. Bosworth, 21, plays Darin's wife—real-life Hollywood actress Sandra Dee. "I knew absolutely nothing about Bobby Darin before this film, but now I'm a huge fan," said Bosworth, who attended the premiere with her British boyfriend Orlando Bloom.

On October 4, 1957, the world witnessed a pivotal moment in history as the Soviet Union launched Sputnik, the world's first artificial satellite, into space. This small, two-foot-diameter metal sphere sparked a space race between the US and USSR that would last for eighteen years and revolutionize our understanding of the world. Interestingly, Sputnik was not the first human-made object to venture into space. That distinction belongs to the V-2 rocket, used by Germany during World War II in missile attacks against Allied cities. Although ineffective, the V-2 rocket technology and its scientists were captured by both the US and USSR at the war's end, and were subsequently repurposed for their own projects.

We have developed single-shot shaped pulses for ultra-high fidelity (uh-fidelity) population transfer on a 3-level quantum system in lambda configuration. To ensure high fidelity, we employ the Lewis-Riesenfeld (L-R) method to derive a family of solutions leading to an exact transfer, where the solutions follow a single dynamical mode of the L-R invariant. Among this family, we identify a tracking solution with a single parameter to control simultaneously the fidelity of the transfer, the population of the excited state, and robustness. We define a measure of the robustness of an uh-fidelity transfer as the minimum percentile deviation on the pulse areas at which the infidelity rises above $10^{-4}$. Our shaped pulses exhibit superior robustness compared to Gaussian and adiabatically-optimized pulses for moderate pulse areas.

This was a great weekend. I spent a lot of time outdoors, reconnecting with nature after a long hiatus, and rediscovered my love for Nevermore's music, something I should have done much sooner. I also managed to get a lot of reading done, finishing three books - Bruce Sterling's Heavy Weather, Larry Niven's World of Ptavvs, and Octavia Butler's Parable of the Sower. Heavy Weather was an engaging cyberpunk adventure set in a near-future USA ravaged by global warming and chaos. Although it was clever, well-paced, and convincingly portrayed, I have to admit that it didn't leave a lasting impression on me. The story and characters were well-crafted and vivid, but overall, it was a good book, rather than a great one.

Roxy Music on Isle of Wight bill: Roxy Music will appear at June's Isle of Wight music festival, along with Morrissey, Supergrass, and Idlewild. R.E.M. have already been confirmed as headliners for the three-day event, which takes place from 10-12 June. Original band members Bryan Ferry, Phil Manzanera, Andy Mackay, and Paul Thompson will take to the stage on the Saturday night of the festival. The band are also working in the studio on new material, their first since the 1982 album Avalon. Roxy Music were formed in 1971 by Ferry, Mackay, and Brian Eno, with Manzanera joining the following year. Their first hit was 1972's 'Virginia Plain', with other hits including 'Street Life', 'Love Is the Drug', and the 1981 John Lennon cover, 'Jealous Guy'.

Weight pruning, a widely recognized technique, has been successfully employed as a straightforward and effective method to eliminate redundancy in deep neural networks (DNNs), thereby achieving accelerated performance on various platforms. However, most pruning methods are essentially trade-offs between model accuracy and regularity, leading to impaired inference accuracy and limited on-device acceleration performance. To address this issue, we introduce a novel sparsity dimension, namely pattern-based sparsity, which encompasses pattern and connectivity sparsity, and achieves both high accuracy and hardware friendliness. By designing carefully crafted patterns, our proposed pruning approach consistently and unprecedentedly enhances accuracy and improves feature extraction capabilities on various DNN structures and datasets, and our pattern-aware pruning framework also facilitates pattern library extraction, pattern selection, pattern, and connectivity pruning, as well as weight training, simultaneously. Our approach, grounded in the new pattern-based sparsity, naturally fits seamlessly into compiler optimization for highly efficient DNN execution on mobile platforms.

Emerging two-dimensional materials with low symmetry—including ReS₂ and ReSe₂ monolayers, phosphorene, group-IV monochalcogenides, and borophene—exhibit far greater structural complexity than conventional honeycomb lattices like graphene, hBN, or transition metal dichalcogenides. This diminished symmetry unlocks remarkable anisotropic phenomena: spatially varying electronic, optical, valleytronic, and spin responses, alongside emergent properties such as ferroelastic/ferroelectric behavior, magnetic ordering, spin-wave dynamics, enhanced nonlinear optics, robust photogalvanic effects, and unconventional superconductivity. Furthermore, reduced symmetry enables exotic electronic topology, nonlinear elasticity, and structural phase transitions absent in high-symmetry systems. This special issue, "Beyond Graphene: Low-Symmetry and Anisotropic 2D Materials," showcases cutting-edge advances in understanding these quantum materials through both experimental discovery and theoretical innovation.

I am recommending this solely because it's free and might be a more enjoyable experience for others. However, for me, the "game" did nothing. Half the story relies on shock value. I was neither shocked nor emotionally moved in any way. Perhaps I'm desensitized, or maybe it was too simple to evoke a reaction from me. The other half of the game attempts to portray (literally) a person in mental decline, dealing with obsession, hallucination, depression, and other issues. In theory, it's a cool idea, but the "game" took me just 10 minutes to complete, even after reading and absorbing every detail possible.

Sun offers processing by the hour. Sun Microsystems has launched a pay-as-you-go service that will allow customers requiring significant computing power to rent it by the hour. Sun Grid costs users $1 (53p) for an hour's worth of processing and storage power on systems maintained by Sun. So-called grid computing is the latest buzzphrase in a company that believes computing capacity is as important a commodity as hardware and software. Sun likened grid computing to the development of electricity. The system could mature in the same way utilities such as electricity and water have developed, said Sun's Chief Operating Officer Jonathan Schwartz. 'Why build your own grid when you can use ours for a buck an hour?' he asked in a webcast launching Sun's quarterly network computing event in California.

Prince Crowned 'Top Music Earner': Prince earned more than any other pop star in 2004, surpassing artists such as Madonna and Elton John in US magazine Rolling Stone's annual list. The singer accumulated $56.5 million (£30.4 million) from concerts, album sales, and publishing, thanks to his Musicology tour and album. He kept Madonna in second place, with her earning $54.9 million (£29.5 million) from her global Re-Invention Tour. Veterans Simon and Garfunkel were in 10th place, with their comeback tour contributing to earnings of $24.9 million (£13.4 million) last year. "Prince returned to center stage after a decade in the commercial wilderness," the magazine reported. The singer's 2004 tour garnered $90.3 million (£48.5 million) in ticket sales, and he sold 1.9 million copies of his latest album, Musicology.

This game was a surprising gem. One often sees rave reviews of certain products that are misleading. In this case, the reviews reflect the fun of this neat little game. It is an odd mix between a classic 2D platformer and a MOBA, and does it with style. I found myself enjoying characters that I wouldn’t enjoy in other MOBA games. I had expected this trait to continue but decided to give a melee tank a go anyway. I was pleasantly surprised with the outcome and ended up trying out more as they were unlocked from playing games. The art style and interface are oddly charming. As such, I now use one of the characters as my avatar and a wallpaper from creating a card set for the game.

The spreadsheet below gives the implicit commodity based on the effective date of four different rates for the same rate schedule (Schedule DR). The rate schedule effective August 2000 through September 2000 yields a much larger commodity charge because SDG&E customers will receive a lump sum reimbursement from SDG&E over those two months. SDG&E customers will expect lower electric bills. Assuming that the lump sum reimbursement is not included in the implicit energy component, the average for June 2000 through January 2001 is 6.2 cts, and the implicit commodity average from February 2001 through December 2001 is 7.3 cts. The average implicit commodity from June 2000 through December 2001 is 6.9 cts. For deliveries starting September 1, 2000, the average would still be 6.9 cts.

Hapoel Bnei Sakhnin, an Israeli football club with a historic presence as the first Arab team to compete in European tournaments, has bolstered its roster with four African talents, including Zimbabwean goalkeeper Energy Murambadoro, for their upcoming UEFA Cup campaign. Their first-round fixture pits them against England’s Newcastle United, marking a significant milestone in the club’s journey.  

Murambadoro, who rose to prominence during the Africa Cup of Nations in Tunisia, played a pivotal role in Bnei Sakhnin’s dominant 6-1 victory over Albania’s Partizani Tirana in the previous qualifying round. The goalkeeper recently joined the Israeli side after a short spell with South Africa’s Hellenic FC.  

Based in the town of Sakhnin, near Haifa, the club has earned recognition not only for its on-field success—culminating in last season’s Israeli Cup triumph—but also for its unwavering commitment to fostering peace and unity within Israel’s diverse society. With a reputation built on integrity and inclusivity, Bnei Sakhnin continues to stand as a beacon of cooperation in the world of sports.

I must start by mentioning that I didn't buy the game "EU" when it was initially released. Instead, I got it during a sale that also included "EW," so I didn't encounter the same issues some players reported with the game. I really enjoyed it; the game was challenging in the beginning but very rewarding once you mastered it. As for "XCOM 2," I absolutely love it. The game runs smoothly for me on medium settings, and I'm using a GTX 560 with 2GB of VRAM. Initially, the game defaulted to high settings, and it ran a bit sluggishly—not enough to be unplayable, but noticeable. When I switched to medium settings, everything ran smoothly. I might just be fortunate with my gaming experiences, but it seems to me that a lot of the negative reviews are coming from people with unrealistic expectations.

Interfacial spin-orbit coupling in Josephson junctions presents a fascinating opportunity to merge anomalous Hall and Josephson physics within a single device. We theoretically investigate how the interplay between these two effects influences the transport properties of superconductor/ferromagnetic insulator/superconductor junctions. The transverse momentum-dependent skew tunneling of Cooper pairs through the spin-active ferromagnetic insulator interface generates substantial transverse Hall supercurrents, which we term anomalous Josephson Hall effect currents. By extending the Furusaki-Tsukada formula, originally developed to quantify conventional Josephson current flows, we evaluate the transverse current components and show that their amplitudes can be broadly tuned through the spin-orbit coupling strengths or the superconducting phase difference across the junction. A distinct spectroscopic signature of Josephson junctions, well-localized subgap bound states form in the vicinity of the interface.

When random effects are correlated with sample design variables, the typical practice of using individual survey weights—designed to be inversely proportional to unit survey inclusion probabilities—to form a pseudo-likelihood no longer leads to asymptotically unbiased inference. We propose a weight-exponentiated formulation for the random effects distribution that enables unbiased inference for generating the hyperparameters of the random effects. In comparison to frequentist methods that depend on numerical integration, our Bayesian approach is unique in achieving both unbiased estimation relative to the sampling design distribution and consistency with the population-generating distribution. Through simulations and a real-world example involving a survey of business establishments, we demonstrate the effectiveness of our method across various modeling frameworks and sampling designs. This work culminates recent advancements combining traditional survey estimation techniques with the Bayesian modeling paradigm, effectively bridging these two rich yet distinct subfields.

We consider the connection between two constructions of the mirror partner for the Calabi-Yau orbifold. This orbifold is defined as a quotient by a suitable subgroup \( g \) of the phase symmetries of the hypersurface \( x_m \) in the weighted projective space, cut out by a quasi-homogeneous polynomial \( w_m \). The first, Berglund-Hübsch-Krawitz (BHK) construction, uses another weighted projective space and the quotient of a new hypersurface \( x_{m^t} \) inside it by a dual group \( g^t \). In the second, Batyrev construction, the mirror partner is constructed as a hypersurface in the toric variety defined by the reflexive polytope dual to the polytope associated with the original Calabi-Yau orbifold. We provide simple evidence for the equivalence of these two constructions.

This game... wow, where do I even begin? It was absolutely stunning, from the story to the graphics—I was completely hooked. According to recent research, high-speed broadband is significantly empowering individuals to express themselves online. A study by the UK think-tank Demos reveals that one in four broadband users in Britain regularly uploads content and maintains personal websites. The report highlights that the availability of constant, fast internet connections is transforming how people engage with the web. Currently, over five million households in the UK are equipped with broadband, and this figure continues to rise rapidly. The Demos report investigates the influence of broadband on users' online behaviors, discovering that more than half of broadband users access the internet before having breakfast. Moreover, one in five users confessed to waking up in the middle of the night specifically to browse the web. The report further emphasizes that broadband is motivating individuals to take a more engaged and participative approach to the online world.

I have always taken pride in making anyone who visits Casa Pescatore feel as comfortable as possible. I want them to feel at home, as they would in their prospective residence. Yesterday, I hope I succeeded. The masses congregated on Perry Avenue with what many considered 'extreme prejudice.' At 4:30 pm, my best friend Eric and his son Nunu arrived with a huge pan of baked ziti. The tables were set, and I had cooked all the food, including the bells ringing crazy. Before I could blink, my best friend from Florida walked through the door, and that made me smile. I missed that motherfucker so much. The bonus was him bringing his wife along, who is expecting and this is the closest I could get to being an uncle. I spoiled that girl with tray after tray of food and tiramisu.

However, aside from a few unique titles, the extensive array of games at CompUSA tends to feel repetitive after a while. Initially, I feel envious, but soon I notice that it's essentially just a myriad of variations on the same theme—first-person shooters, mystery games, strategy games, and simulations. The endless versions of "Age of Empires" become almost laughable: "AOE with orcs," "AOE with spaceships," "AOE with fuzzy wombats." It's reminiscent of the countless movie trailers that begin with, "In a world...

Menstruation  a natural biological process, shrouded in centuries of stigma, is often whispered about. But why? As I speak to you now, over 800 million women worldwide are experiencing it. None of us would exist without it, yet it remains an "embarrassing" subject to discuss openly. From my experience and that of the women around me, I can attest that it's exhausting. It's exhausting to carefully extract a discreet tampon from a brown paper bag, tucking it into your pocket during a class, and rushing to the washroom, all while attempting to appear normal. It's exhausting to sit through lessons and meetings, pretending to be completely unaffected, while internally struggling with intense period cramps.

The El Paso pipeline has announced an open season to test market interest in a mainline expansion and to satisfy regulatory concerns that need to be addressed to relieve the apparent capacity constraint at the California border. El Paso has been under significant pressure from regulators and shippers to expand due to enormous demand and skyrocketing prices in California. In a January 3 letter to the pipeline, Daniel M. Adamson, Director of FERC's Office of Energy Projects, urged El Paso to consider modifying its Line 2000 project to "assist the difficult situation" now facing the California gas market.

Magic fans, beware: Wizards seems hesitant to create a decent digital game. At its core, Magic: The Gathering is a social experience meant to be enjoyed with friends, sharing laughter, trash talk, and game-winning moments. Unfortunately, Wizards of the Coast appears to have lost sight of this. When playing with friends, you're unable to earn gold, which is a crucial aspect of the game. Gold is so vital that a daily cap has been implemented, likely to prevent the unlocking of all in-game content. However, be prepared for a lengthy and monotonous 1v1 grind against the computer or random opponents, with whom you can't even communicate. If you opt to play for free, expect to spend around 4 hours a day, solo, for over a month to unlock the cards.

Warframe is an engaging game for the initial hours of play. However, beyond that, you may start to notice its repetitive nature in combat, enemies, and overall gameplay. A notable strength is the dedication of its developers, who frequently release updates packed with substantial new content, from extensive system changes to new warframes and other enhancements. It's an enjoyable game for casual play, although it might struggle to keep your interest for more than a few months unless you particularly enjoy grinding. The advantage is that it's free to play, so I recommend giving it a try. If you're in need of a quick gaming experience, it's worth checking out.

An electron is usually considered to have only one type of kinetic energy, but could it have more, due to its spin and charge, or by exciting other electrons? In one dimension (1D), the physics of interacting electrons is captured well at low energies by the Tomonaga-Luttinger-liquid (TLL) model, yet little has been observed experimentally beyond this linear regime. Here, we report measurements of many-body modes in 1D gated wires using a tunnelling spectroscopy technique. We observe two separate Fermi seas at high energies, associated with spin and charge excitations, together with the emergence of three additional 1D 'replica' modes that strengthen with decreasing wire length. The effective interaction strength in the wires is varied by changing the amount of 1D inter-subband screening by over 45%.

Manchester rock band Doves have soared to the top of the UK album chart with their new release, Some Cities, entering at number one. The trio replaces flamboyant US act Scissor Sisters at the top. The album follows the single Black and White Town, which reached number six. R&B star Nelly has the new number one single with Over and Over, a collaboration with Tim McGraw. Girls Aloud, Akon, and Kaiser Chiefs all have new singles in the top ten, alongside The Futureheads and Usher. The latest Elvis Presley re-release, (Marie's the Name) His Latest Flame, entered the chart at number three, one place ahead of Girls Aloud's Wake Me Up. Hip-hop performer Akon's Locked Up is at number five, while hotly-tipped Leeds band Kaiser Chiefs have their second chart hit at number six with Oh My God.

Electric network frequency (ENF) fluctuations serve as a potent tool in multimedia forensics. A novel approach to ENF estimation is presented, leveraging temporal windowing based on the filter-bank Capon spectral estimator. By exploiting the Toeplitz structure of the model covariance matrix, a type of Gohberg-Semencul factorization is employed. Notably, this approach pioneers the use of a non-rectangular temporal window in the pre-spectral estimation stage within the ENF field. Krylov matrices facilitate efficient matrix inversions, enabling fast implementation. The proposed method surpasses state-of-the-art ENF estimation techniques when utilizing a 1-second time window in power recordings. Furthermore, it yields highly accurate results in speech recordings, striking a balance between time complexity and accuracy.

I have always prided myself in making anyone that frequents Casa Pescatore as comfortable as possible. I want them to feel at home, as they would in their prospective residence. Yesterday—I hope I succeeded. The masses all congregated on Perry Ave with what many consider 'extreme prejudice.' 4:30 PM was the time my best friend Eric and his son Nunu came over with a huge pan of baked ziti. The paper proposes a new effective theory of baryons, which includes a novel Skyrme-like conserved current based on the idea of hidden local symmetry. The proposed current is called the "baryon current" and is related to the Skyrme charge, but with a different value. The current is defined as equivalent to the Skyrme charge for any smooth configuration, but with a different value in the presence of the new symmetry.

The proposal is based on the idea that the baryon current can be expressed as a linear combination of the vector mesons and the chern-simons fields. The chern-simons fields are the vector fields that are dual to the gluons, and the vector mesons are the meson fields that are charged under the new symmetry. The baryon current is proposed to be a linear combination of these fields, with a coefficient that is determined by the new symmetry.

The proposal gives a unified picture for the two types of baryons, which are the proton and the neutron, and allows them to continuously transform into each other in a natural way. The baryon current is proposed to be the effective current for the baryons at low energies, and it is expected to be a good approximation for the full theory at high energies as well.

The proposal also includes a dual interpretation of the chern-simons fields

Chris Anderson: Welcome back to the TED community for another live conversation, one that promises to be a pivotal moment in our collective journey. As we launched the 'Build Back Better' series, our primary objective was to explore ways to address the challenges that have arisen in the wake of the pandemic and to reimagine a more resilient future. However, the tragic events of the past week, including the devastating loss of George Floyd and the subsequent wave of protests, have injected a sense of urgency that demands our immediate attention. As we grapple with the question of whether we can truly build back better from this moment, it's essential that we first seek to comprehend the profound magnitude of this crisis. Whitney Pennington Rodgers: Indeed, Chris.

The Weak Gravity Conjecture (WGC) posits that the existence of super-extremal particles is a necessary condition for any consistent quantum theory of gravity. The standard lore suggests that these particles are introduced to ensure that extremal black holes are either unstable or marginally stable, but the implications of this assumption are unclear if it does not occur. This note reveals that, for a generic Einsteinian quantum theory of gravity in Anti-de Sitter space, the stability of extremal black branes is in tension with rigorously proven quantum information theorems about entanglement entropy. To avoid this contradiction, a non-perturbative version of the WGC is required, which reduces to the usual statement at weak coupling. The argument is general and does not rely on either supersymmetry or a particular UV completion, assuming only the validity of Einstein's gravity, effective field theory, and holography.

The **direpack** package is designed to introduce a collection of modern statistical dimension reduction techniques into the Python ecosystem as a unified and cohesive toolkit. The included dimension reduction methods fall into three main categories: projection pursuit-based approaches, sufficient dimension reduction, and robust M-estimators for dimension reduction. Additionally, the package offers regularized regression estimators built upon these reduced-dimension spaces, spanning from traditional principal component regression to sparse partial robust M-regression.  

Beyond core functionality, **direpack** provides a suite of classical and robust preprocessing utilities, such as generalized spatial signs, along with specialized plotting tools and cross-validation support. Designed for seamless integration, the package adheres to the **scikit-learn** API, ensuring compatibility with statistical and machine learning pipelines within that framework.

Do you remember "Age of Mythology?" It's been quite a while since we've seen an RTS game of similar caliber. However, Divinity: Dragon Commander comes remarkably close to being one of my personal favorites in the RTS genre. It combines this with an expansive, Risk-like grand strategy mode that perfectly complements the experience. I strive to remain objective, but it's hard not to be impressed by a game that lets you transform into a dragon, assisting your troops through support, attacks, and even directing unit production, movement, and spawning simultaneously. While I appreciate the complexity of modern RTS games like Civilization V, these titles often try to pack numerous intricate mechanics, graphs, and resources into a single, convoluted gameplay mode.

The China Three Gorges Project Corporation is defying government orders to halt construction on one of its giant dams, despite facing fines, according to Chinese state media. The company is continuing work on the Xiluodu Dam, one of 30 large-scale construction projects that were ordered to cease operations due to a lack of proper environmental assessments. The Three Gorges Dam builder has also ignored orders to halt construction at two other projects, the Three Gorges Underground Power Plant and the Three Gorges Project Electrical Power Supply Plant. So far, only 22 of the 30 targeted projects have complied with the shutdown order from China's State Environmental Protection Administration (SEPA).

Robinson Aims for Dual Code Success: England rugby union captain Jason Robinson is aiming for dual code success against Australia this Saturday. Robinson, a former rugby league international before switching codes in 2000, will lead England in their match against Australia at Twickenham at 14:30 GMT. Later, at 18:15 GMT, Great Britain's rugby league team will face Australia in the final of the Tri-Nations Tournament. "Beating the Aussies in both games would be a massive achievement, especially for the league," said Robinson. England has the opportunity to secure their third autumn international victory after consecutive wins over Canada and South Africa, and to avenge June's 51-15 defeat by the Wallabies. Meanwhile, Great Britain could end 34 years of losses against Australia with a victory at Elland Road.

Iraq invites phone license bids: Iraq is inviting bids for two telephone licenses, aiming to significantly boost nationwide coverage over the next decade. Bids have been invited from local, Arab and foreign companies, Iraq's Ministry of Communications said. The winner will work in partnership with Iraq's telecommunications and postal company (ITPC). The firms will install and operate a fixed phone network, providing voice, fax and internet services. The ministry aims to increase Iraq's "very low telephone service penetration rate from around 4.5% today to about 25% within 10 years. It also hopes to develop a "highly visible and changeable telecommunications sector". Details of the bidding and tender process will be published on the ministry's website on February 9. Additionally, it is planning a road-show for investors in Amman, Jordan.

Last night in class, we discussed the week’s assigned reading: *Getting Back into Place: Toward a Renewed Study of the Place-World* by Edward S. Casey. The first chapter examines "implacement," while the second explores "displacement." In the opening chapter, Casey argues that contemporary culture prioritizes time as the central organizing force—evident in phrases like "running out of time," "saving time," or "just in time." He proposes, instead, that centering culture around place fosters a more grounded and connected community. (This is a rough summary—disclaimer in case my professor or classmates come across this post.) So far, this book is the first assigned reading that has truly captivated me.

It's been a challenging week, marked by a bout of tendonitis in my right shoulder, specifically beneath the shoulder blade. The condition is exacerbated by repetitive arm movements, particularly when using a computer mouse. Unfortunately, a recent marathon session of updating my website and attending to other online tasks has left me struggling to muster the strength to engage in even simple activities like drawing. Compounding the issue is a lack of sleep, which has intensified the discomfort. Despite my current frustration, I remain optimistic about a full recovery. On a more positive note, my mother's surgery was successful, and she has begun to experience a partial restoration of her eyesight. While her vision remains limited, she can now discern fuzzy shapes and distinguish colors, offering a glimmer of hope that she will eventually regain her full visual acuity.

Warning over tsunami aid website: Net users are being advised to avoid a scam website that claims to collect cash on behalf of tsunami victims. The site appears plausible as it uses an old version of the official Disasters Emergency Committee webpage. However, the DEC has no connection with the fake site and has contacted the police about it. The site is the latest in a long list of scams attempting to cash in on the goodwill generated by the tsunami disaster. The link to the website is contained in a spam email currently circulating, with the subject line "Urgent Tsunami Earthquake Appeal" and text that features poor grammar and bad spelling, characteristic of many other phishing attempts.

It’s 1988, and scientist James Hansen has just testified to the United States Congress that global warming trends are caused by human activity, and will pose an increasing threat to humanity in the future. Well, well. That’s unusually prescient for a human. Looking for a wedding dress? Try a new take on a timeless classic. It’s sleek, flattering, and modest—just like the traditional dress. Commercials. Could anything be more insufferable? It’s 1997, and the United States Senate has called a hearing about global warming. Some expert witnesses point out that past periods in Earth’s history were warmer than the 20th century.

Some people are jumping to connect the dots: the Organization for Security and Cooperation in Europe was invited by the State Department to monitor the election. The observers will come from the OSCE's Office for Democratic Institutions and Human Rights. This will mark the first time such a team has been present for a U.S. presidential election. Meanwhile, a baker's dozen of congressional Democrats, including four New Yorkers, has petitioned U.N. Secretary-General Kofi Annan to dispatch international observers to Florida to oversee this year's presidential balloting there. I humbly disagree. Based on the available information, the OSCE appears to be coming to learn from the U.S. election process, rather than to oversee it, which was the original partisan and childish call for U.N. intervention.

Andrea Levy, the Orange Prize winner, has seen her book 'Small Island' win the Whitbread Novel of the Year award. She is now the favourite to win the overall prize after beating Booker winner Alan Hollinghurst's 'The Line of Beauty'. Geraldine McCaughrean has picked up the Children's Fiction award for the third time for 'Not the End of the World'. All category winners will compete for the Whitbread Book of the Year title, which carries a £25,000 prize. A panel of judges, including Sir Trevor McDonald, actor Hugh Grant, and writer Joanne Harris, will meet on 25 January to decide the overall winner, with the announcement being made later that evening. Bookmaker William Hill has placed London-based Levy's novel as the 6/4 favourite to win.

This paper investigates the optimization landscape of population negative log-likelihood functions in Gaussian mixture models with an arbitrary number of components. While these models exhibit nonconvexity—resulting in numerous suboptimal local minima even in well-separated cases—we demonstrate that all local minima possess a consistent structural pattern. Specifically, each local minimum partially identifies true component centers through either: (1) multiple Gaussians approximating a single true component, or (2) a single Gaussian approximating multiple true components. Our analysis holds under specific separation conditions between true components and remains applicable regardless of whether the model correctly specifies the number of components. Furthermore, for three-component Gaussian mixtures, we establish more precise results that quantify the relationship with inter-component separation distances.

A cross-party coalition of parliamentarians has intensified the push for reform of the House of Lords by releasing a comprehensive proposal for its transformation. The draft legislation, unveiled by the group, envisions a streamlined second chamber, with 70% of its members elected by the people. This move comes as a response to the long-standing stalemate on reform, which has persisted since 1999, when the removal of 600 hereditary peers from the House of Lords was implemented. Despite the impasse, the group remains optimistic about garnering support for the abolition of the remaining 92 hereditary peers. The government had previously shelved plans to eliminate these hereditary peers, citing the unlikelihood of success due to opposition from within the House of Lords. Prime Minister Tony Blair has consistently emphasized the need for consensus on reform, suggesting that any changes to the House of Lords, including potential modifications to its powers, may be outlined in the Labour Party's forthcoming manifesto.

Understanding how strongly correlated two-dimensional (2D) systems can give rise to unconventional superconductivity with high critical temperatures is one of the major unsolved problems in condensed matter physics. Ultracold 2D Fermi gases have emerged as clean and controllable model systems to study the interplay of strong correlations and reduced dimensionality, but direct evidence of superfluidity in these systems has been missing. Here, we demonstrate superfluidity in an ultracold 2D Fermi gas by moving a periodic potential through the system and observing no dissipation below a critical velocity \( v_{\rm c} \). We measure \( v_{\rm c} \) as a function of interaction strength and find a maximum in the crossover regime between bosonic and fermionic superfluidity. Our measurement establishes ultracold Fermi gases as a powerful tool for studying the influence of reduced dimensionality on strongly correlated superfluids.

Where does all the carbon we release go? Carbon works in a natural cycle, present in all living things: in the soil, in the oceans, in the atmosphere, and in rocks deep underground. When plants and other living things die, much of their stored carbon makes its way back to the atmosphere until new plants grow and reabsorb it. But over millions of years, some of the carbon stored in ancient trees and sea life becomes rock, and some becomes fossil fuels like coal, oil, and natural gas. Natural events, such as volcanic eruptions, release some of the carbon trapped in rock. Human activities, like burning fossil fuels, also release some of that prehistoric carbon. Today, humans release around 60 times more carbon than all the volcanoes on the planet each year.

The reboot of the Devil May Cry franchise has brought about many changes for the series, particularly for the series’ protagonist, Dante. While fans of the original series may be upset at the change to their beloved character, it’s certainly for the better; and this new game by Ninja Theory proves it. DmC serves as the perfect point for newcomers to jump into the series. It shows the origins of an arrogant, cynical Dante, and it chronicles his journey from cynicism to selflessness. Throughout, the game weaves a clear narrative arc for the new Dante, making him a compelling character to watch. The story follows Dante as he’s introduced into the corrupt world he willfully chose to ignore.

Yukos, a Russian oil company, is set to take legal action against Baikal Finance Group, a little-known firm, in a US court on Wednesday to seek sanctions for the $20 billion damages it claims it suffered due to the sale of Yuganskneftegas. Yukos will argue that Baikal and other parties involved in the sale ignored a US court order last week, which blocked the sale. The Russian government's decision to ignore the court order has raised questions about Baikal's motives for buying the unit. Russian newspapers have reported that Baikal, which purchased the Yuganskneftegas production unit for $9.4 billion (261 billion rubles, £4.8 billion) on Sunday at a state-arranged auction, has strong ties with Surgutneftegas, Russia's fourth-largest oil producer.

Optomechanics and electromechanics have made it possible to prepare macroscopic mechanical oscillators in their quantum ground states, in quadrature squeezed states, and in entangled states of motion. This new capability has enabled the idea of using mechanical oscillators in the processing and communication of quantum information and as precision force sensors operating beyond the standard quantum limit. However, the effectively linear interaction between motion and light or electricity precludes access to the broader class of quantum states of motion, such as cat states or energy squeezed states. Early optomechanical proposals noted the possibility to escape this restriction by creating strong quadratic coupling of motion to light. Although there have been experimental demonstrations of quadratically coupled optomechanical systems, these have not yet accessed non-classical states of motion.

Understanding how two-dimensional (2D) systems can exhibit unconventional superconductivity with high critical temperatures is one of the major unsolved problems in condensed matter physics. Ultracold fermi gases have emerged as clean and controllable model systems to study the interplay of strong correlations and reduced dimensionality, but direct evidence of superfluidity in these systems has been missing. Here, we demonstrate superfluidity in an ultracold 2D fermi gas by moving a periodic potential through the system and observing no dissipation below a critical velocity v$_{\rm c}$. We measure v$_{\rm c}$ as a function of interaction strength and find a maximum in the crossover regime between bosonic and fermionic superfluidity. Our measurement establishes ultracold fermi gases as a powerful tool for studying the influence of reduced dimensionality on strongly correlated superfluids.

We present a novel mixed variable symplectic (mvs) integrator for planetary systems that resolves close encounters with unprecedented accuracy. Our method combines a time regularization technique with a high-order mvs scheme, allowing for large time steps far from close encounters while maintaining stability and energy conservation. We demonstrate the algorithm's ability to resolve almost exact collisions with a fraction of the physical radius separation, while using the same time step as in weakly perturbed problems such as the solar system. We test the long-term behavior of our algorithm on systems of six super-Earths experiencing strong scattering over 50 kyr, and compare it to hybrid methods such as Mercury. Our approach achieves the same level of accuracy at a lower computational cost.

As I begin my poetry workshops, I pose two fundamental questions to my students  'Whose shoulders do you stand on?' and 'What do you stand for?' These inquiries are crucial because, at times, poetry can seem like a seemingly inaccessible art form, often associated with an elitist past, as embodied by figures like Benjamin Franklin or other historical literary giants. However, I share my own response to these questions, which is a powerful declaration that I repeat before every performance  'I am the daughter of black writers, descended from freedom fighters who broke their chains and reshaped the world.' These words serve as a personal mantra, a reminder of my heritage and the responsibility that comes with it. In fact, I was rehearsing them in the corner over there, making sure to embody the power and conviction behind them.
