An electron is typically considered to have only one type of kinetic energy, but could it have more, due to its spin and charge, or by exciting other electrons? In one dimension (1D), the physics of interacting electrons is well captured at low energies by the Tomonaga-Luttinger-Liquid (TLL) model, yet little has been observed experimentally beyond this linear regime. Here, we report on measurements of many-body modes in 1D gated wires using a tunnelling spectroscopy technique. We observe two separate Fermi seas at high energies, associated with spin and charge excitations, along with the emergence of three additional 1D 'replica' modes that strengthen with decreasing wire length. The effective interaction strength in the wires is varied by changing the amount of 1D inter-subband screening by over 45%.

For the past 24 years, I have served as a firefighter in Huntington, West Virginia. My team and I are responsible for protecting lives and property from disasters such as car accidents, house fires, and life-threatening medical emergencies. As a woman leading a department in a male-dominated profession, I’ve faced unique challenges. A decade ago, I chose to expand my medical expertise by earning a nursing degree. This decision came from recognizing that the next major threat to my city—and communities nationwide—wasn’t the single, dramatic crisis where firefighters arrive, resolve the situation, and depart with a sense of accomplishment. Instead, it was the growing need for sustained medical care and preparedness.

After the first week back from practicum, I experienced a challenging aftermath, but it wasn't until the second week that the haze of uncertainty finally began to clear. The post-practicum trauma that I had been carrying was only now coming to light. I was initially overwhelmed, but a meeting with the course coordinator allowed me to release my burden, and in doing so, I was able to prevent another student from being stuck with a pedagogically deficient practicum experience. Yesterday, on Monday, I ran into another agitated classmate who was determined to warn a fellow student about the difficulties they would face in the upcoming practicum. I calmed her down and suggested that we should report to the administrator first, together.

Weight pruning has been widely recognized as a straightforward and effective method for eliminating redundancy in deep neural networks (DNNs), thereby achieving acceleration on various platforms. However, most pruning techniques are essentially trade-offs between model accuracy and regularity, leading to impaired inference accuracy and limited on-device acceleration performance. To address this issue, we introduce a novel sparsity dimension, namely pattern-based sparsity, which combines pattern and connectivity sparsity, and proves to be highly accurate and hardware-friendly. With carefully designed patterns, our approach consistently achieves accuracy enhancement and better feature extraction ability on different DNN structures and datasets, and our pattern-aware pruning framework also enables pattern library extraction, pattern selection, pattern, and connectivity pruning and weight training simultaneously. Our approach naturally fits seamlessly into compiler optimization for highly efficient DNN execution on mobile platforms.

As I stand before you, I wonder what thoughts cross your mind. Do you see a woman of unwavering faith, a knowledgeable expert, or perhaps a kindred spirit? Or do you perceive me as oppressed, brainwashed, or even a threat to society? Maybe, in a more mundane sense, you associate me with the frustration of a delayed airport security line – a notion that, I must confess, holds a grain of truth. If some of your initial impressions were less than favorable, I don't hold it against you. The media has, after all, played a significant role in shaping these perceptions, with a staggering 80 percent of news coverage about Islam and Muslims being decidedly negative. It's no surprise, then, that many Americans admit to not knowing a Muslim personally. I often joke that perhaps they just don't engage in conversation with their Uber drivers. To those of you who have never had the opportunity to meet a Muslim, I'm delighted to make your acquaintance.

Continuous-time quantum walks have proven to be an extremely valuable framework for designing various quantum algorithms. The running time of quantum algorithms within this framework is typically characterized by the quantum hitting time  the time required for the quantum walk to find a vertex of interest with a high probability. In this article, we present improved upper bounds for the quantum hitting time that can be applied to several continuous-time quantum walk-based quantum algorithms. Specifically, we apply our techniques to the glued-trees problem, significantly improving their hitting time upper bound from O(n^5) to O(n^2 log n). Moreover, our methods also enable exponential improvements in the precision of the continuous-time quantum walk-based algorithm to locate a marked node on any ergodic, reversible Markov chain, as demonstrated by Chakraborty et al.

Jason Robinson, the captain of England's rugby union team, is aiming for a significant dual-code victory against Australia this Saturday. Having transitioned from rugby league to rugby union in 2000, Robinson will lead England as they face Australia at Twickenham at 14:30 GMT. Later, at 18:15 GMT, Great Britain's rugby league team will meet Australia in the Tri-Nations tournament final. Robinson stated, "Defeating the Aussies in both matches would be a tremendous accomplishment, particularly for rugby league." England is poised to secure their third autumn international win following successful matches against Canada and South Africa, and they hope to avenge their 51-15 loss to the Wallabies in June. Simultaneously, Great Britain has the opportunity to break a 34-year win drought against Australia with a victory at Elland Road.

While not perfect, this game has been a thoroughly enjoyable experience. However, the save system and the possibility of sudden death can lead to some frustration, particularly during the early stages of the game. I experienced this firsthand when I lost my main character around the 10th hour of my 20-hour playthrough. A save feature to reload at that point would have been greatly appreciated. Fortunately, I've since discovered a third-party mod/save tool to mitigate the risk of catastrophic failures. Additionally, the aiming reticle could be slightly larger and the zoom function could be more powerful. Despite these minor drawbacks, I've thoroughly enjoyed my time with the game. As someone who doesn't typically play zombie-themed games, I've found the combination of strategy, missions, and first-person shooter/beat-em-up elements to be particularly engaging. I've found myself playing it as often as I can get away, often getting sucked into just one more mission or supply run before reluctantly shutting the game down.

Prince was crowned the 'top music earner' in 2004, outpacing other pop stars including Madonna and Elton John in US Magazine's annual list. The singer raked in an impressive $56.5 million (£30.4 million) from concerts, album, and publishing sales during his Musicology Tour and album. He maintained his lead over Madonna, who earned $54.9 million (£29.5 million) while embarking on her global re-invention tour. Veteran duo Simon and Garfunkel placed 10th, their comeback tour contributing $24.9 million (£13.4 million) to their earnings last year. Prince returned to the spotlight after a decade in the commercial wilderness, according to the magazine, with his 2004 tour generating $90.3 million (£48.5 million) in ticket sales and 1.9 million copies of his latest album, Musicology, being sold.

We establish natural splittings for the values of global Mackey functors at orthogonal, unitary, and symplectic groups. In particular, the restriction homomorphisms between the orthogonal, unitary, and symplectic groups of adjacent dimensions are naturally split epimorphisms. The interest in these splittings stems from equivariant stable homotopy theory, as the equivariant stable homotopy groups of every global spectrum form a global Mackey functor. Consequently, the splittings imply that certain long exact homotopy group sequences decompose into short exact sequences. For the real and complex global Thom spectra $\mathbf{mo}$ and $\mathbf{mu}$, the splittings imply the regularity of various Euler classes related to the tautological representations of $O(n)$ and $U(n)$.

'To the moon' is no trivial pursuit... Nay, it is an unparalleled experience that has left an indelible mark on my life. The likes of Telltale's The Walking Dead pale in comparison, for this masterpiece represents the zenith of narrative craftsmanship. The soundtrack, a harmonious and enchanting accompaniment, is a testament to the game's poetic genius. Although the controls can be temperamental and the gameplay may not be as polished, the narrative's spellbinding nature rendered any technical shortcomings inconsequential. The game's acerbic wit and clever references to Doctor Who are but a bonus, adding an extra layer of depth and sophistication to an already transcendent experience. In short, 'To the moon' is a true work of art that will leave you both depressed and fulfilled in the most profound ways.

There are hundreds of thousands of people on transplant waiting lists, desperately in need of critical organs like kidneys, hearts, and livers that could be the difference between life and death. Unfortunately, the number of available donor organs is woefully insufficient to meet the staggering demand. What if, instead of waiting for a match, we could create brand-new, custom-designed organs from scratch? That is the innovative concept behind bioprinting, a cutting-edge branch of regenerative medicine currently under development. While we are not yet able to print complex organs, we are making significant strides in creating simpler tissues, such as blood vessels and tubes responsible for facilitating nutrient exchange and waste elimination, which are crucial for the survival of the human body. Bioprinting is a biological cousin of 3D printing, a technique that deposits layers of material on top of each other to construct a three-dimensional object one layer at a time.

Have you ever experienced one of those moments where you pause to reflect on life? Perhaps you're sitting in a math class, feeling utterly bored, and you start to wonder what processes in your brain enable you to comprehend math. You then realize your existence and the fact that you're in math class, prompting you to question the purpose of it all. This realization can be quite profound, as you begin to appreciate the beauty of simply existing. However, this train of thought is often short-lived, as you suddenly feel a pang of embarrassment at the randomness of your musings. It's a feeling that can be quite frustrating. On a related note, I have a strong dislike for block schedules. Who would conceive of such an idea? It's clear that sitting through a two-and-a-half-hour class in any subject is a form of cruel and unusual punishment.

British stars denied major Oscars. British hopes of winning major Oscars were dashed as UK stars failed to win acting and directing prizes. Despite three nominations, Mike Leigh's Vera Drake failed to take the director or screenplay awards, and there was no prize for star Imelda Staunton. Kate Winslet also lost to Best Actress Hilary Swank, while Clive Owen and Sophie Okonedo failed to win Best Supporting Actor awards. The UK's Andrea Arnold won the short film award for her gritty drama Wasp. Arnold, who presented children's television shows Number 73 and Motormouth in the 1980s, said it was "totally overwhelming" to win. Wasp tells the story of a single mother living on the breadline, beating films by fellow UK nominees Gary McKendry and Ashvin Kumar.

Warframe is an enjoyable game for the initial few hours of play, offering an engaging experience. However, as time progresses, the game's limitations become apparent, with repetition in combat, enemies, and gameplay becoming the primary focus. A notable exception is the developers' dedication to regular updates, which often include substantial content additions, such as significant system overhauls, new warframes, and various tweaks and features. This makes Warframe suitable for casual play. Nevertheless, its ability to sustain long-term interest is questionable, unless you have a strong affinity for grinding. On the positive side, the game is free, making it worth trying out, especially if you're looking for a brief gaming fix.

Wales 'must learn health lessons' from England, says new Health Minister. Dr Brian Gibbons, on his first full day in the job following Jane Hutt's dismissal, acknowledged "big challenges" but maintained that the "essentials" were in place. However, both Plaid Cymru and the Liberal Democrats argued that Dr Gibbons needed to change policy. Meanwhile, Ms Hutt defended her record, stating that waiting times and lists comprised "only 10% of the health agenda." Dr Gibbons, a former GP in Blaengwynfi, Upper Afan Valley, and AM for Aberavon, said NHS staff desired a period of consolidation after "tremendous change and reform." One of the major issues that had plagued Ms Hutt during her five-and-a-half-year tenure as the assembly's first Health Minister was the lengthy waiting lists in Wales.

Low-symmetry 2D materials, such as res$_2$ and rese$_2$ monolayers, black phosphorus monolayers, group-IV monochalcogenide monolayers, borophene, and others, exhibit more complex atomic structures compared to the honeycomb lattices of graphene, hexagonal boron nitride, and transition metal dichalcogenides. The reduced symmetries of these emerging materials lead to inhomogeneous electron, optical, valley, and spin responses, as well as novel properties such as ferroelasticity, ferroelectricity, magnetism, spin-wave phenomena, large nonlinear optical properties, photogalvanic effects, and superconductivity. Additionally, novel electronic topological properties, nonlinear elastic properties, and structural phase transformations can occur due to low symmetry. The "Beyond Graphene: Low-Symmetry and Anisotropic 2D Materials" special topic highlights recent experimental and theoretical research on these emerging materials.

Novel concepts, perspectives, and challenges in measuring and controlling an open quantum system through sequential schemes are presented. We discuss how similar protocols, employing both repeated quantum measurements and dynamical decoupling control pulses, can: (i) confine and protect quantum dynamics from decoherence, in line with Zeno physics; (ii) analytically predict the probability that a quantum system is transferred into a target quantum state via stochastic sequential measurements; and (iii) optimally reconstruct the spectral density of environmental noise sources by orthogonalizing the filter functions driving the designed quantum sensor in the frequency domain. Achieving these tasks will enhance our ability to observe and manipulate open quantum systems, thereby advancing quantum science and technologies.

I cannot envision anyone who solely dedicates themselves to a single pursuit in life, as the vast majority of individuals possess diverse interests and passions. According to recent statistics, around 15% of American workers do not hold traditional, full-time jobs. Instead, they engage in half-time, part-time, contract, or temporary work arrangements. This shift towards non-traditional employment aligns with the ethos of the term "side hustle," which refers to the practice of juggling multiple income streams to supplement one's primary source of earnings. The origins of the term "side hustle" can be traced back to popular African American newspapers in the 1920s, where it was used to describe a scam. However, by the 1950s, the term had evolved to encompass legitimate work endeavors as well. A side hustle differs from a second job in that it is not solely focused on financial necessity, but rather on exploring various interests and passions.

Ireland surged past Scotland at Murrayfield, maintaining their six-nation Grand Slam ambitions with an impressive victory. Hugo Southwell's try gave the Scots an early 8-0 lead, but scores from locks Malcolm O'Kelly and Paul O'Connell put the visitors in command by halftime. A third try from wing Denis Hickie and a third penalty from Ronan O'Gara, who kicked 13 points, extended the lead. Jon Petrie scored a second try for Scotland, but late scores from John Hayes and Gavin Duffy sealed the victory. After two hard-earned away victories, Eddie O'Sullivan's side can now look forward to welcoming England to Lansdowne Road in a fortnight. Scotland will try to give their coach Matt Williams a first Six Nations victory when Italy come to Edinburgh, but they struggled to turn pressure into points.

Deadline Nears for Fiat-GM Deal

Fiat and General Motors (GM) have until midnight on February 1 to resolve a disagreement over a potential takeover. The deadline marks the point at which Fiat will gain the right to sell its car division to GM, a part of an alliance agreed upon in 2000. GM, whose own European operations are losing money, no longer wants to own the unprofitable Fiat unit. Reports of deadlocked talks sent Fiat shares down 1.2% on Tuesday, following Monday's 4% gain on hopes of a payoff. The U.S. firm is thought to be offering about $2 billion (£1.06 billion) to extricate itself from the arrangement. It has argued the deal was voided by Fiat's decision to sell off its finance arm and halve GM's stake via a capital-raising effort. The 2000 deal resulted from a race between GM and DaimlerChrysler to ally with Fiat.

In this paper, we investigate the landscape of the population negative log-likelihood function of Gaussian mixture models with an arbitrary number of components. Due to non-convexity, there exist multiple local minima that are not globally optimal, even when the mixture is well-separated. We show that all local minima share the same form of structure, which involves a non-overlapping combination of fitting multiple Gaussians to a single true component and fitting a single Gaussian to multiple true components. Our results are valid for the setting where the true mixture components satisfy a certain separation condition, and are sharper than previous results when the number of components is over- or under-specified. For Gaussian mixtures with three components, we obtain sharper results in terms of the scaling with the separation between the components.

I had initially acquired this game in its early development phase, when the primary currency was spacebux, and there was no 'tech tree' in place. During this period, I found the game to be enjoyable. All the weapons at this stage were equally overpowered, resulting in a lack of balance; anyone could eliminate anyone at any moment. Additionally, the game initially provided unlimited gun slots and five loadouts. One might assume that, given the vast array of 'bajillions of guns', the developers would have offered more than six weapon slots and three loadouts before requiring additional payment. In reality, I had amassed a collection of approximately three pages worth of firearms, and was exploring new ideas for expansion, as I found it most enjoyable when I had a surplus of options. This experience led me to believe that the game's developers could have done better in terms of providing a more balanced gameplay experience.

Electric network frequency fluctuations constitute a powerful tool in multimedia forensics. An efficient approach for ENF estimation is introduced, utilizing a novel temporal windowing strategy based on the filter-bank capon spectral estimator. A type of Goerg-Semencul factorization of the model covariance matrix is employed due to the Toeplitz structure of the covariance matrix. Moreover, this approach innovatively applies a non-rectangular temporal window, at the stage preceding spectral estimation, a departure from traditional rectangular windowing. Krylov matrices are leveraged for rapid implementation of matrix inversions. The proposed approach outperforms state-of-the-art methods in ENF estimation, when a short time window of 1 second is employed in power recordings. In speech recordings, the proposed approach yields highly accurate results with respect to both time complexity and accuracy.

Deep hashing techniques have demonstrated remarkable retrieval efficiency and accuracy in large-scale image retrieval systems. The focus of deep hashing methods has always been on optimizing discrete hash codes. These methods typically employ an activation function—such as $\operatorname{sigmoid}(\cdot)$ or $\operatorname{tanh}(\cdot)$—and aim to minimize a quantization loss to approximate discrete values. However, this approach can lead to an increasing number of hash bits getting stuck in the incorrect saturation zones of the activation functions, from which they cannot escape. We refer to this issue as the "dead bits problem" (dbp). Moreover, the traditional quantization loss can exacerbate dbp. In this paper, we introduce a straightforward yet effective solution: a gradient amplifier that operates before the activation functions to mitigate dbp.

A new report suggests that reducing the drink-drive limit could save more lives than previously thought. According to a study by University College London, cutting the limit from 80mg of alcohol per 100ml of blood to 50mg would result in 65 lives saved annually. The findings are being published by the Parliamentary Advisory Council for Transport Safety as MPs prepare to vote on the government's road safety bill. The bill includes changes to speeding fines, but not to the drink-drive limit. The research, conducted by transport expert Professor Richard Allsop, also suggests that this move would lead to 230 fewer serious injuries on the UK's roads compared to 2003, resulting in estimated annual savings of £119m. A previous estimate, contained in a 1998 Department of Transport document, had suggested that such a move could save 50 lives a year.

Magic fans, be warned... Wizards seems hesitant to create a truly engaging digital game. At its heart, Magic: The Gathering is a social experience—a game meant to be shared with friends (or frenemies), filled with laughter, playful banter, and game-changing plays. Unfortunately, Wizards of the Coast appears to have lost sight of this. If you opt to play with friends, you miss out on earning gold, which is crucial in-game. Gold is so vital that they’ve even imposed a daily cap—understandable, since it unlocks nearly everything. But brace yourself for a tedious, solitary grind in 1v1 matches against AI or silent strangers. If you’re playing for free, expect to spend roughly four hours daily for over a month, alone, just to unlock the cards.

Despite my enthusiasm for free-to-play games and indie developers, I was disappointed to find that this particular title fell short of my expectations. The game's description had initially resonated with me, as I have a fondness for puzzle and exploration games. However, upon playing, I discovered that the execution was marred by a lack of intuitive design, making it difficult for me to progress even after seeking guidance. Moreover, I felt that the game's emphasis on cutscenes overshadowed the actual gameplay, resulting in lengthy periods of inactivity that left me feeling unengaged.

Weight pruning is widely recognized as a straightforward and effective method to reduce redundancy in deep neural networks (DNNs), thereby achieving acceleration on various platforms. However, most pruning techniques essentially involve trade-offs between model accuracy and regularity, leading to impaired inference accuracy and limited on-device acceleration performance. To address this issue, we introduce a new sparsity dimension, called pattern-based sparsity, which includes both pattern and connectivity sparsity, making it both highly accurate and hardware-friendly. With carefully designed patterns, the proposed pruning consistently achieves unprecedented accuracy enhancement and better feature extraction capabilities across different DNN structures and datasets. Our pattern-aware pruning framework also simultaneously performs pattern library extraction, pattern selection, pattern and connectivity pruning, and weight training. Our approach to the new pattern-based sparsity naturally fits into compiler optimization for highly efficient DNN execution on mobile platforms.

Deep learning has revolutionized the field of problem-solving, tackling complex challenges that were previously insurmountable for heuristic algorithms. Notably, its successful application in wireless communications has yielded significant benefits, even in areas where existing radio systems are well-established and optimal algorithms are readily available. While learning individual components of a receiver has led to some improvements, a more effective approach involves jointly learning the entire receiver pipeline. However, this holistic approach often gives rise to a formidable nonlinear problem, whose optimal solution is impractical to implement. To address this challenge, we introduce Deeprx, a novel deep fully convolutional neural network that seamlessly integrates the entire receiver pipeline, from frequency domain signal processing to uncoded bit extraction, in a manner compliant with 5G standards. By carefully crafting the input to the convolutional neural network using a combination of data and pilot symbols, we enable accurate channel estimation, thereby enhancing the overall performance of the receiver.

The world's largest and most devastating environmental and industrial project is located in the heart of the largest and most intact forest in the world, Canada's Boreal Forest. It stretches across northern Canada, home to the largest remaining wild caribou herd in the world, the George River caribou herd, numbering approximately 400,000 animals. Unfortunately, I couldn't find any of them during my visit, but you have their antlers as proof. All across the Boreal, we're blessed with this incredible abundance of wetlands. Wetlands are one of the most endangered ecosystems globally, providing critical ecological services such as cleaning the air, cleaning water, sequestering large amounts of greenhouse gases, and housing a vast array of species.

Cardinal Cormac Murphy-O'Connor has criticised the billions of pounds spent on the Iraq war and conflicts in the Middle East, stating that the funds could have been better utilised to alleviate poverty. The head of the Catholic Church in England and Wales expressed his views on BBC Radio 4 and is expected to reiterate his stance during his Christmas midnight mass. In a Christmas message, the cardinal denounced the Iraq war as a "terrible" waste of resources, echoing sentiments previously shared by the Archbishop of Canterbury. During his appearance on BBC Radio 4's Thought for the Day, he lamented the fact that vast sums have been spent on war, rather than being invested in initiatives to lift people out of dire poverty, malnourishment, and disease. The cardinal urged that 2005 should be a year of concerted effort to "make history poverty".

I encountered no performance issues even at launch, and with all the subsequent patches, the game now runs exceptionally well. In my opinion, it’s genuinely a fantastic and enjoyable game, though the format can be slightly frustrating at times—particularly how the free-roam city repopulates with enemies after each story progression. The 'dual play' segments, where you control Batman alongside another character, were a standout highlight. I also appreciated the flexibility of using a controller for most gameplay while switching to mouse and keyboard for tank combat sections. Navigating the Batmobile through the open city—especially during APC chases—becomes incredibly satisfying once you master using the brakes to drift around corners. Once you get the hang of it, cruising through Gotham is an absolute delight!

Someone once remarked that politics is essentially "show business for the unattractive." By that measure, I suppose I’ve truly made it. Another point worth considering is what a privilege it is, as a politician, to deliver a TED Talk—especially here in the U.K., where the standing of politics has plummeted so dramatically since the expenses scandal.  

In fact, I recently came across a story about scientists contemplating replacing lab rats with politicians in their experiments. When someone asked why, the response was: "Well, there’s no shortage of politicians, few people care what happens to them, and let’s face it—there are some things even rats refuse to do." *(Laughter)*

Arnesen denies rift with Santini. Tottenham sporting director Frank Arnesen has denied that coach Jacques Santini resigned because of a clash of personalities at White Hart Lane. There had been newspaper speculation that Santini had felt undermined by Arnesen's role at the club. "It is absolutely not true," Arnesen told BBC Radio Five Live. "There is only one thing that made him resign, and that is his own personal problems." He has talked to me recently and said this matter is absolutely for himself. Arnesen said he was unable to throw any light on the problems that caused Santini to quit after just 13 games in charge. He added: "Jacques has never gone into exactly what it was, but I trust him in that; you have to accept it. I think we should respect it.

Good evening, everyone. I'm Simone. You've probably heard the age-old advice for stage fright: imagine the audience naked—supposedly to ease your nerves. But let's be honest: visualizing all of you undressed in 2018 feels outdated, even inappropriate. We're striving for progress, leaving such antiquated notions behind. So, I propose a fresh approach to handling performance anxiety.  

Here’s my revelation: true comfort would come from balance—if I could meet your gaze just as intently as you watch me. Imagine if I had an abundance of eyes—then we’d all be on equal footing, wouldn’t we? A room full of mutual observation, where no one feels exposed or uneasy. That’s the harmony I’d choose.

Hollywood stars Kevin Spacey and Kate Bosworth attended the British premiere of their new film, 'Beyond the Sea', at London's Leicester Square on Thursday. Spacey, 45, wrote, directed and starred in the film, paying tribute to the life of 1950s crooner Bobby Darin. "This is my tribute to someone I think was a remarkable talent," Spacey said, who, as Darin, sings all 18 songs on the film's soundtrack. Bosworth, 21, plays Darin's wife - real-life Hollywood actress Sandra Dee. "I knew absolutely nothing about Bobby Darin before this film, but now I'm a huge fan," Bosworth said, who attended the premiere with her British boyfriend, Orlando Bloom.

They basically fixed *Shadow Warrior* (2013). The boss fights are awesome, weapons feel very nice (maybe not as good as *D44M*'s, but still solid), and enemies aren't too spongy—unless you're careless with handling elemental and upgrade mechanics. The upgrade system is well-balanced and not too tedious once you get the hang of it. The elemental effects and limited ammo gently encourage you to switch between weapons. There are lots of cool, unique weapons (key word here, unlike *Borderlands*' RNG-based formula) that remain viable for most of the game. The music is great, especially the main theme and, of course, Stan Bush's tracks.

Spontaneous conversations in real-world settings, such as those in child-centered recordings, are among the most challenging audio files to process. However, developing speech processing models capable of handling this wide range of conditions would be particularly beneficial for language acquisition studies. Researchers are interested in both the quantity and quality of speech that children hear and produce, as well as for early diagnosis and measuring the effects of remediation. In this paper, we present our approach to designing an open-source neural network to classify audio segments into vocalizations produced by the child wearing the recording device, vocalizations from other children, adult male speech, and adult female speech. To achieve this, we gathered diverse child-centered corpora totaling 260 hours of recordings, covering 10 languages.

Yukos Seeks Court Action on Sale: Yukos will return to a U.S. court on Wednesday to seek sanctions against Baikal Finance Group, the little-known firm that has bought its main asset. Yukos has stated it will sue Baikal and others involved in the sale of Yuganskneftegas for $20 billion in damages. Yukos' U.S. lawyers will attempt to have Baikal's assets frozen after the Russian government ignored a U.S. court order last week blocking the sale. Baikal's background and its motives for buying the unit remain unclear. Russian newspapers have claimed that Baikal—which purchased the Yuganskneftegas production unit for $9.4 billion (261bn roubles, £4.8bn) on Sunday at a state-provoked auction—has strong links with Surgutneftegas, Russia's fourth-biggest oil producer.

In 1903, the president of the United States took a three-day camping trip in California’s Yosemite Valley. President Theodore Roosevelt slept in a grove of towering sequoia trees, camped in a snowstorm, and spent hours talking around the campfire with his host and guide, conservationist John Muir. Roosevelt famously loved the outdoors, but Muir had invited him there for more than just camping: Yosemite was in danger. Though Yosemite became protected land in 1864, the valley was still at risk of overdevelopment in 1903. It was at the heart of a decades-old struggle to set aside land for both preservation and public use—two goals that were much easier said than done. The battle over Yosemite began with the 1849 Gold Rush, when miners surged west, seeking gold in the Sierra Nevada foothills.

The only solution was to restart the client, which was not a major inconvenience. Causal inference from observational data is a primary objective of numerous data analyses in the health and social sciences. Historically, however, academic statistics has been skeptical of data analyses with a causal focus. The emergence of the term "data science" presents a unique opportunity to redefine data analysis in a way that seamlessly integrates causal inference from observational data. Similar to previous frameworks, we categorize the scientific contributions of data science into three primary tasks: description, prediction, and counterfactual prediction, which encompasses causal inference. A clear classification of data science tasks is essential for discussing the data, assumptions, and analytics required to successfully accomplish each task. We contend that the failure to adequately acknowledge the role of subject-matter expert knowledge in data analysis is a significant source of widespread misconceptions about data science.

I'm feeling uncomfortable with the way investigative reporting is portrayed in this article. While I appreciate the importance of investigative reporting, I cannot help but feel that the consequences of pursuing such reporting are not entirely justifiable. Undertale masterfully blends its elements to create an experience that transcends its individual components. While the pixel-art visuals are charmingly nostalgic and the soundtrack is delightful on its own, their synergy produces moments of breathtaking beauty and emotional resonance. The writing effortlessly shifts between humor, drama, sorrow, and optimism—often simultaneously—while cleverly subverting traditional RPG conventions. This praise doesn't even begin to address the game's rich world-building, memorable characters, compelling narrative, or innovative gameplay mechanics, each worthy of extensive analysis. But let me simply say this: Whether you're an RPG enthusiast, a general gaming aficionado, or simply someone with a few spare hours in your lifetime, Undertale is an essential experience you won't want to miss.

Subject: Notice of CFTC Approval of New NYMEX Rule 6.40C

Date: October 3, 2000

To all NYMEX and COMEX Members,

From: Neal L. Wolkoff, Executive Vice President

I am writing to inform you that the Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) has approved the implementation of NYMEX Rule 6.40C, titled "Average Price Calculations by Futures Commission Merchants." This newly approved rule, which aligns with a recent CFTC advisory, is now in effect. 

Rule 6.40C allows Futures Commission Merchants (FCMs), under certain specified conditions, to utilize their own internal, proprietary systems for generating Average Price (AP) calculations. This applies when multiple prices are received on a single order or a series of orders. The full details of this rule are attached for your reference. 

Please review this information carefully to ensure compliance. 

Thank you.

Debra Hill, a screenwriter and producer known for her work on the 1970s horror classic "Halloween," has passed away in Los Angeles at the age of 54. Hill, who had been battling cancer, co-wrote the 1978 film that featured Jamie Lee Curtis as a babysitter terrorized by a psychopath. Directed by John Carpenter, the film grossed over $60 million (£31.3 million) worldwide, setting a record for independent films at the time. Hill also collaborated with Carpenter on "Escape from New York" and "The Fog." Born in New Jersey, Hill started her career as a production assistant, eventually rising through the ranks to become an assistant director and second-unit director before her partnership with Carpenter. She was considered a pioneering woman in the film industry, taking on roles in the 70s that were predominantly held by men.

Where does all the carbon we release go? Carbon works in a natural cycle, present in all living things: in the soil, in the oceans, in the atmosphere, and in rocks deep underground. When plants and other living things die, much of their stored carbon makes its way back to the atmosphere until new plants grow and reabsorb the carbon. However, over millions of years, some of the carbon stored in ancient trees and sea life becomes rock and some becomes fossil fuels: coal, oil, and natural gas. Natural events, such as volcanic eruptions, release some of the carbon trapped in rock. Meanwhile, human activities, like burning fossil fuels, also release some of that prehistoric carbon. Today, humans release around 60 times more carbon than all the volcanoes on the planet each year.

Sustainability encompasses the what, where, and how of seafood production. However, it's the who and why that truly matter to me. I'm interested in learning about the people behind my dinner choices, understanding how my decisions impact them, and how they, in turn, impact me. I want to know their motivations for fishing and how they rely on the ocean's resources for their livelihood. By gaining this insight, we can shift our perspective on seafood from a commodity to an opportunity to restore our ecosystem. This allows us to appreciate the seafood we're fortunate to eat, while also promoting a more positive relationship with the environment. I believe this approach can be described as restorative seafood. While sustainability focuses on endurance and maintenance, restorative seafood is about replenishing and progressing, ultimately creating a more sustainable future.

Subject: Notification to All PX Market Participants

Date: Monday, December 11, 2000  
From: John Yurkanin, Chief Operating Officer

Re: Disruption in PX Day-Ahead Auction Due to ISO Rule Changes

Last Friday's emergency tariff filing by the California ISO has adversely affected the PX day-ahead auction market. The combination of soft and hard caps leads to a complete disconnect, posing unacceptable risks to all participants. This situation is unlikely to assist the ISO in achieving its intended decrease in real-time energy sales and procurement. As a result, suppliers will likely continue to defer to the higher price opportunities offered by the ISO. The PX is actively petitioning Terry Winter of the ISO to allow for immediate adjustment bids on any submitted schedule at any relevant price.

Roxy Music will perform at June's Isle of Wight Music Festival, alongside artists such as Morrissey, Supergrass, and Idlewild. REM has already been confirmed as the headliner for the three-day event, taking place from June 10-12. Original band members Bryan Ferry, Phil Manzanera, Andy Mackay, and Paul Thompson will take the stage on Saturday night of the festival. The band is also working in the studio on new material, their first since the 1982 album Avalon. Roxy Music was formed in 1971 by Ferry, Mackay, and Brian Eno, with Manzanera joining the following year. Optomechanics and electromechanics have enabled the preparation of macroscopic mechanical oscillators in quantum ground states, quadrature-squeezed states, and entangled motional states. Beyond pushing larger, more tangible objects into the quantum regime, this advancement has spurred proposals to utilize mechanical oscillators for quantum information processing, communication, and high-precision force sensing beyond the standard quantum limit. However, the predominantly linear interaction between motion and light or electricity restricts access to a wider range of quantum motional states, such as cat states or energy-squeezed states. Early optomechanical proposals highlighted the potential to overcome this limitation by establishing strong quadratic coupling between motion and light. While experimental realizations of quadratically coupled optomechanical systems exist, they have yet to achieve nonclassical states of motion.

The reboot of the Devil May Cry franchise has introduced several changes to the series, particularly affecting its protagonist, Dante. MPs to lords reform plan a group of MPs has tried to raise the pressure on Tony Blair over reform to the House of Lords by publishing a detailed blueprint for change. The cross-party group has unveiled a draft bill proposing a smaller second chamber in which 70% of members would be elected. MPs and peers have failed to agree on reform since 1999 when 600 hereditaries lost their seats. The group says it can win support for removing the last 92 hereditaries. The government postponed plans to remove the remaining hereditary peers because they said they were unlikely to succeed after opposition in the Lords. Tony Blair has argued that there needs to be consensus on reforms. There have been suggestions that there will be proposals for changing at least the powers of the Lords in Labour's manifesto.

An electron is typically considered to have only one type of kinetic energy, but could it possess more due to its spin and charge, or by exciting other electrons? In one dimension (1D), the physics of interacting electrons is well described at low energies by the Tomonaga-Luttinger liquid (TLL) model, yet little has been experimentally observed beyond this linear regime. Here, we report measurements of many-body modes in 1D gated wires using a tunneling spectroscopy technique. We observe two distinct Fermi seas at high energies, associated with spin and charge excitations, along with the emergence of three additional 1D 'replica' modes that intensify as the wire length decreases. The effective interaction strength in the wires is varied by adjusting the amount of 1D inter-subband screening by over 45%.

What is a parent? It's a question that is not easily answered. Today, we have a complex definition of parenthood, encompassing adoption, stepfamilies, and surrogate mothers. Many parents face difficult decisions and questions about how to approach conversations with their children. For instance, should they reveal the story of sperm donation to their child? If so, when is the right time to do so, and what words should they use? The term "biological father" is often used to refer to sperm donors, but is it truly an accurate description? As a philosopher and social scientist, I have delved into these questions about the concept of parenthood. However, today, I will share with you what I've learned from speaking with parents and children. I will demonstrate that what matters most in a family is not necessarily the biological ties, but rather the love, care, and commitment that exists between family members, regardless of their family structure.

The study presents observations of thermally induced transverse vibrations in a photonic crystal waveguide (PCW). The waveguide is composed of two parallel nanobeams separated by a 240 nm vacuum gap. Developed models have been validated for converting beam movements into phase and amplitude modulation of a weak optical probe traveling in a guided mode (GM) of the PCW, applicable both when probe frequencies are distant from and near the dielectric band edge. Given that this PCW is designed for near-field atom trapping, the research lays the groundwork for assessing potential negative impacts of thermal motion on optical atomic traps located close to PCW surfaces. The long-term objectives include establishing robust atom-mediated interactions between individual vibrational phonons and single photons traveling in the waveguide's GMs, thus facilitating quantum-level opto-mechanical interactions involving atoms, photons, and phonons.

Do you ever have one of those moments when you start reflecting on life? Like you're sitting in math class, bored out of your mind, and you wonder what exactly happens in your brain that helps you understand math. Then you realize that you exist, you're in math class, and you ask yourself why. You start to appreciate how amazing existence is. Suddenly, it hits you—this random train of thought is incredibly pathetic. Ever had that happen? I hate that. You know what else I hate? Block schedules. Who came up with that idea? Anyone can see that sitting through a two-and-a-half-hour class in any subject is cruel and unusual punishment.

This paper introduces a millimeter-scale CMOS 64$\times$64 single charged particle radiation detector system designed for external beam cancer radiotherapy. A 1$\times$1 $\mu m^2$ diode is employed to measure the energy deposited by a single charged particle within the depletion region. The array offers a substantial detection area of 512$\times$512 $\mu m^2$. Instead of detecting the voltage drop due to radiation, the system captures the pulse width, which is the duration for the voltage to return to its baseline. This approach eliminates the need for power-intensive and bulky analog-to-digital converters. A prototype ASIC has been fabricated using the TSMC 65 nm LP CMOS process, with an average static power consumption of 0.535 mW under a 1.2 V analog and digital power supply. The entire system's functionality is successfully validated in a clinical setting using a 67.5 MeV proton beam.

After completing the game, my stats indicate that it took me 8.6 hours to reach the end, with 10 collectibles found out of 25. While I appreciated the visuals throughout the game, I was disappointed by the lackluster storyline. However, the ending was somewhat redeeming, with a unique and unexpected twist that made me feel less disappointed with my time invested. Unfortunately, the last level was a letdown, with uninteresting visuals and increasingly difficult gameplay that made me feel frustrated and eager to move on from the experience. Of the 15 achievements, I was able to unlock only a few, which I found to be too challenging and not worth the effort. Overall, I would recommend this game with reservations, as it has some enjoyable elements but ultimately falls short of its potential.

This paper demonstrates that the eccentric debris rings observed around stars Fomalhaut and HD 202628 are narrower than expected in the standard eccentric planet perturbation scenario, also known as the "pericenter glow." According to this scenario, an initially circular and narrow belt of planetesimals is assumed to exist at semi-major axis $a$, whose eccentricity is increased to $e_f$ after secular perturbations from an eccentric planet, resulting in a belt of width $2ae_f$. However, a minor modification of this scenario can produce narrower belts if the planetesimals are initially eccentric, which could arise from earlier planet perturbations during the gas-rich protoplanetary disc phase. Alternatively, a primordial eccentricity could be caused by instabilities that increase the disc eccentricity without the need for any planets.

Continuous-time quantum walks have proven to be an extremely useful framework for designing several quantum algorithms. Often, the running time of quantum algorithms in this framework is characterized by the quantum hitting time: the time required for the quantum walk to find a vertex of interest with a high probability. In this article, we provide improved upper bounds for the quantum hitting time that can be applied to several CTQW-based quantum algorithms. In particular, we apply our techniques to the glued-trees problem, improving their hitting time upper bound by a polynomial factor: from $o(n^5)$ to $o(n^2\log n)$. Furthermore, our methods also help to exponentially improve the dependence on precision of the continuous-time quantum walk-based algorithm to find a marked node on any ergodic, reversible Markov chain by Chakraborty et al.

Bastion is a truly exceptional game, boasting a captivating storyline. Set in a post-calamity world, you embark on rebuilding the bastion. The game offers a considerable amount of choice, allowing you to decide what to build to gain an advantage, which weapons to wield, and what power-ups to equip them with, as well as selecting drinks that grant bonus effects. The art style and music are fantastic, with vibrant visuals that defy the notion that a post-apocalyptic world must be dull. One of the standout features is the dynamic narration, which responds to the player's actions, such as switching between weapons in the armoury, prompting the narrator to comment on the new choice.

Quantum walks in continuous time have emerged as a highly effective framework for the design of numerous quantum algorithms. The running time of these algorithms is often characterized by the quantum hitting time, which is the time required for the quantum walk to find a vertex of interest with a high probability. In this study, we provide improved upper bounds for the quantum hitting time that can be applied to various quantum algorithms based on continuous-time quantum walks. Notably, we apply our techniques to the glued-trees problem, significantly reducing their hitting time upper bound by a polynomial factor  from O(n^5) to O(n^2logn). Moreover, our methods also enable exponential improvements in the precision of the continuous-time quantum walk-based algorithm to find a marked node on any ergodic, reversible Markov chain, as demonstrated by Chakraborty et al.

However, I believe that too much energy was spent on the set, which was magnificent, and not enough on creating a solidifying tone, theme, etc. The story was retold, but it wasn't particularly gripping, and I don't feel like I gained anything from watching it. This is an absolute great strategy game! Having played every single other war game (European Escalation and Albion), I have to say this one fits well. The number of units is great. Not sure exactly how many units there are, but there's a crap ton! Graphics are just beautiful, sounds are great, and the gameplay overall is epic. The in-game chat box at the main menu is very entertaining, no, I'm not kidding. However, if you think this game is 'easy', or if you're new to the strategy genre, it could take you quite some time to get it nailed down. Don't get upset, you will lose a lot of units. As I attempted to do laundry with the kids in tow today, I was struck by how quickly the simplest tasks can become overwhelming. I was also pleasantly surprised by the kindness of strangers, who were sweet to me for no apparent reason other than their genuine desire to be kind. It wasn't as if they were trying to solicit something in return; they simply wanted to be friendly. I had a moment where I had to deal with a drooling child, and I was wearing my designated 'wash-day' outfit. Furthermore, I'm not sure I'll ever feel the need to bathe again anytime soon, so there was no real incentive to rush through the process. As I'm doing two things simultaneously, I'm writing this post and watching a collection of videos at my desk in my room.

A fundamental principle in quantum gravity, the weak gravity conjecture, necessitates the presence of superextremal particles in any theoretically consistent framework. Conventionally, these particles are thought to play a crucial role in ensuring that extremal black holes exist in a state of instability or marginal stability. However, the underlying rationale for this requirement remains unclear. This analysis reveals that, within the context of a generic Einsteinian quantum theory of gravity in anti-de Sitter space, the exact stability of extremal black branes is inherently at odds with rigorously established quantum information theorems governing entanglement entropy. Resolving this paradox yields a nonperturbative formulation of the weak gravity conjecture, which converges to the conventional statement under weak coupling conditions. Notably, this argument is universally applicable, independent of supersymmetry or specific ultraviolet completions, and relies solely on the validity of Einsteinian gravity, effective field theory, and holographic principles.

The Milky Way's assembly history is being progressively revealed through the insights gained from the Gaia mission. It is believed that the globular cluster system of our galaxy has undergone a similarly complex formation process. To better understand this formation path, our focus is on exploring the relationship between the globular clusters and the known merging events that have shaped the Milky Way. For this purpose, we merged the kinematic data from Gaia, covering nearly all galactic clusters, with a comprehensive dataset of cluster ages that have been meticulously adjusted for any systematic errors. By examining their dynamical properties, particularly within the framework of integrals of motion, we seek to identify clusters that share a common origin. Our findings indicate that approximately 40% of these clusters likely originated from within the galaxy itself.

This paper introduces a millimeter-scale CMOS 64$\times$64 single charged particle radiation detector system designed for external beam cancer radiotherapy. A 1$\times$1 $\mu \text{m}^2$ diode is used to measure energy deposition by a single charged particle in the depletion region, and the array design achieves a substantial detection area of 512$\times$512 $\mu \text{m}^2$. Instead of detecting the voltage drop induced by radiation, the proposed system measures the pulse width—essentially, the duration it takes for the voltage to return to its baseline. This approach eliminates the need for power-intensive and sizable analog-to-digital converters. A prototype ASIC is fabricated using the TSMC 65 nm LP CMOS process, consuming an average static power of 0.535 mW under a 1.2 V analog and digital power supply. The system's functionality has been successfully verified in a clinical 67.5 MeV proton beam setting.

We introduce a novel Skyrme-like conserved current within the effective theory of pions and vector mesons, grounded in the concept of hidden local symmetry. The corresponding charge is equivalent to the skyrmion charge for any smooth configuration. Furthermore, singular configurations exist that can be identified as n_f=1 baryons, which are charged under this new symmetry. Under this identification, vector mesons assume the role of Chern-Simons vector fields residing on the quantum Hall droplet that constitutes the n_f=1 baryon. We propose that this current provides the correct effective expression for the baryon current at low energies. This proposal offers a unified framework for the two types of baryons, enabling a natural and continuous transformation between them. Additionally, Chern-Simons dualities on the droplet can be interpreted as a manifestation of Seiberg-like duality between gluons and vector mesons.

I know this is going to sound strange, but I think robots can inspire us to be better humans. See, I grew up in Bethlehem, Pennsylvania, the home of Bethlehem Steel. My father was an engineer, and when I was growing up, he would teach me how things worked. We would build projects together, like model rockets and slot cars. Here's the go-kart that we built together. That's me behind the wheel, with my sister and my best friend at the time. And one day, he came home, when I was about 10 years old, and at the dinner table, he announced that for our next project, we were going to build... a robot. A robot.

Do I even need to explain why Portal is amazing? Portal is a puzzle game that presents you with a concept and then lets you explore it. I still play this game and complete it whenever I'm feeling bored, and I love revisiting it to see what gave birth to a legend and what has become a legend. Gameplay: Portal's gameplay is quite simple to explain, at least. You use portals to your advantage, along with buttons that activate parts of the puzzle. There are two types of buttons in the game: one that you press and one that requires a cube to activate.

Mgrigsb.nsf? Over the weekend, Gov. Davis and Senate Polanco made an informal agreement whereby Senate Polanco has agreed to sponsor the SoCal MOU bill. Polanco is a Democrat and is currently suffering through a personal scandal; as a result, it is reported that his political career is essentially dead. Polanco is planning to run for mayor of Los Angeles rather than seek another Senate term.  

The bill is expected to be introduced in the new extraordinary session, beginning today or tomorrow. The bill would first go to the Senate Energy Committee. Sen. Bowen, the chair of the committee, strongly opposes the MOU, so the bill is expected to remain in committee hearings for some time. The longer the bill is tied up in committee politics and changes are made, the likelihood of a SoCal bankruptcy increases.

I am sorry I cannot show you my face, because if I do, the bad guys will come for me. My journey started 14 years ago. I was a young reporter. I had just come out of college. Then I got a scoop. The scoop was a very simple story. Police officers were taking bribes from hawkers who were hawking on the streets. As a young reporter, I thought that I should do it in a different way, so that it would have a maximum impact, since everybody knew that it was happening, and yet there was nothing that was keeping it out of the system. So I decided to go there and act as a seller. As part of selling, I was able to document hardcore evidence. The impact was great. It was fantastic. This was what many call immersion journalism, or undercover journalism.

Despite reported interest from the Ospreys, Mefin Davies is expected to remain with Gloucester Rugby, a development that would come as a relief to the club and its fans. According to BBC Wales, the 32-year-old hooker is keen to extend his stay at Kingsholm, where he has established himself as a key player. Having starred in Saturday's RBS Six Nations victory over England, Davies is likely to be offered a new contract by the Zurich Premiership side. The Welsh international's future was thrown into uncertainty following the demise of the Celtic Warriors region in the summer, which led to him taking a semi-professional contract with Neath RFC. Although he gained valuable experience with the Ospreys at the request of the Wales management, Davies has since spoken candidly about his frustration with the circumstances surrounding his departure from the region. His impressive performances for Gloucester have since earned him a prominent place in the team, and it is anticipated that he will be rewarded with a new deal in the coming months.

In this paper, we explore multi-agent discrete-event systems with the challenge of partial observation. We categorize the agents into distinct groups, within which agents share similar (isomorphic) state transition structures. Consequently, they can be reconfigured into a unified template. Utilizing this template, we develop a scalable supervisor designed for partial observation scenarios. Notably, the state size and computational cost of this supervisor remain constant, regardless of the number of agents in the system. The scalable supervisor under partial observation is robust, eliminating the need for recalibration as agents are added or removed. We extend our prior work to address partial observation by introducing sufficient conditions that ensure both safety and maximal permissiveness of the scalable least restrictive supervisor at the template level. An example is included to demonstrate the proposed method for scalable supervisory synthesis.

England's dual-code star, Jason Robinson, has set his sights on dual-code success against Australia on Saturday. As a former rugby league international, Robinson has switched codes in 2000 and will lead England against Australia at Twickenham at 1 30pm GMT. England will look to seal their third autumn international victory, following consecutive wins over Canada and South Africa, and exact revenge for their 51-15 hammering by the Wallabies in June. Beating the Australians in both games would be a massive achievement, especially for league, and England have the chance to put an end to 34 years of failure against Australia with a victory at Elland Road.

While not perfect, this game has been incredibly enjoyable. The save system and risk of sudden death can be frustrating at times. During my first playthrough, I lost my main character around the 10-hour mark of a 20-hour campaign, and I would have appreciated the option to reload from a previous save. To mitigate such losses, I've resorted to using a third-party mod and save tool. Additionally, the aiming reticule is somewhat small, and the zoom feature could be more powerful. Despite these issues, the game has been a blast. As someone who isn't typically a fan of zombie games, I've been drawn in by the combination of strategy, missions, and first-person shooter and beat-em-up elements. I find myself playing as often as possible, always tempted to complete just one more mission or supply run before shutting down the game.

Podcasting has revolutionized the way people consume music on their Apple iPods or other digital music players. With a storage capacity of up to 10,000 songs, it's no wonder that many podcasters are taking advantage of this space to fill. Led by former MTV host and VJ (video jockey) Adam Curry, podcasting has become a popular way for creators to produce and share their own audio content. The term 'podcasting' is derived from the Apple iPod, although it's not necessary to have an iPod to create or listen to a podcast. Essentially, a podcast is an internet-based radio show that podcasters produce, often from the comfort of their own homes. All they need is a microphone, a PC, and some editing software to create their shows, which they then upload to the internet, making them available for free download and listening.

The developers significantly improved *Shadow Warrior (2013)*—boss battles are exhilarating, and the weapon mechanics are satisfying (though perhaps not as refined as *DOOM 2016*'s, they remain well-crafted). Enemy durability is well-balanced, provided you skillfully manage elemental effects and upgrades. The upgrade system is thoughtfully designed, avoiding unnecessary complexity while maintaining depth. Limited ammunition and elemental interactions subtly encourage varied weapon usage. The game also features numerous distinct weapons—far more meaningful than *Borderlands*' reliance on randomized loot—that remain effective throughout the experience. The soundtrack is exceptional, particularly the main theme and, unsurprisingly, Stan Bush's iconic contributions.

The widespread adoption of broadband internet is revolutionizing the way people express themselves online, with a significant proportion of users in the UK leveraging fast web access to share their thoughts, ideas, and creativity with the world. According to a comprehensive report by the UK-based think-tank Demos, nearly a quarter of broadband subscribers in Britain regularly upload content and maintain personal websites, underscoring the profound impact of high-speed internet on online behavior. The always-on, fast connection afforded by broadband is transforming the internet experience, enabling users to engage with online content in new and innovative ways. With over five million households in the UK already connected to broadband and this number growing rapidly, the report's findings offer a fascinating glimpse into the evolving online habits of the British public. Notably, the study reveals that more than half of broadband users begin their day by logging onto the web, while a significant minority even confess to rising in the middle of the night to browse the internet. Perhaps most importantly, the report argues that broadband is empowering individuals to take a more active and participatory role in the online community, fostering a culture of creativity, self-expression, and digital engagement.

You use portals to your advantage, along with buttons that activate a part of the puzzle. Two buttons are in the game, one that you press and one that you need a cube to place on.

My journey to become a polar specialist, photographing and specializing in the polar regions, began when I was four years old, when my family moved from southern Canada to northern Baffin Island, near Greenland. There, we lived with the Inuit in a tiny Inuit community of 200 people, where we were one of three non-Inuit families. In this community, we didn't have a television, computers, or radio, and we didn't even have a telephone. As a result, I spent all my time outside with the Inuit, playing. The snow and ice were my sandbox, and the Inuit were my teachers. That's where I became truly obsessed with the polar realm.

Magic fans, beware... Wizards seems reluctant to create a truly satisfying digital game. At its heart, Magic: The Gathering is a social game meant to be enjoyed with friends (or frenemies), filled with laughter, banter, and dramatic game-winning moves. Wizards of the Coast appears to have forgotten this. If you decide to play with friends, you can't earn gold—and gold is crucial. It's so vital that they've set a daily cap, which is understandable since gold unlocks everything in the game. But brace yourself for a long and monotonous 1v1 grind against the computer or random opponents, whom you can't even communicate with. If you opt to play for free, it will take you roughly 4 hours a day for over a month, by yourself, to unlock the cards.

I am writing to you today as a non-privileged update, and I want to acknowledge that the past couple of weeks have been particularly challenging. I hope that our daily updates on new developments have been helpful in providing clarity on the current state of our company. In this regard, I would like to take a moment to recap the key events from last week. Notably, we successfully executed a commitment letter with JPMorgan and Salomon Smith Barney, securing additional credit lines totaling $1 billion. These assets will be supported by our Northern Natural Gas Company and Transwestern Pipeline Company. This development brings our total credit lines to $4 billion, which we intend to utilize to retire short-term commercial debt and maintain our liquidity position. Furthermore, I am pleased to announce that our Board of Directors has elected a new member, William Powers, Jr., the esteemed Dean of the University of Texas School of Law in Austin, Texas.

Sustainability represents the essence, location, and method of what is caught. The who and the why behind it are what's crucial to me. I want to know the people behind my dinner choices. I want to know how I impact them. I want to know how they impact me. I want to know why they fish. I want to know how they rely on the ocean's bounty for their survival. Understanding all of this enables us to shift our perspective on seafood from a commodity to an opportunity to restore our ecosystem. It allows us to appreciate the seafood we're so fortunate to consume. So, what do we call this? I think we call it restorative seafood. Where sustainability is the capacity to endure and maintain, restorative is the ability to replenish and progress.

Scott5.nsf ahimsa (uh-him-sah) noun the principle of noninjury to living beings. [Sanskrit ahimsa : a-, not + himsa, injury (from himsati, he injures).] 'As my conception of ahimsa went on maturing, I became more vigilant about my thought and speech. The lines in the anthem: "Scatter her enemies and make them fall; confound their policies, frustrate their knavish tricks" particularly jarred upon my sentiments of ahimsa.' M.K. Gandhi, The Story of My Experiments with Truth, 1927. Today is October 2, the day Mohandas Karamchand Gandhi was born in the year 1869. A man born some two centuries ago may appear irrelevant to our age, but in reality his life and message are still applicable to our lives today.

We developed single-shot shaped pulses for ultra high fidelity (uh-fidelity) population transfer in a three-level quantum system with a lambda configuration. To ensure high fidelity, we use the Lewis-Riesenfeld (L-R) method to derive a family of solutions that lead to an exact transfer, where these solutions follow a single dynamical mode of the L-R invariant. Within this family, we identify a tracking solution with a single parameter that simultaneously controls the fidelity of the transfer, the population of the excited state, and robustness. We define a measure of robustness for an uh-fidelity transfer as the minimum percentile deviation in the pulse areas at which the infidelity exceeds $10^{-4}$. The robustness of our shaped pulses is superior to that of Gaussian and adiabatically-optimized pulses for moderate pulse areas.

The world's largest and most devastating environmental and industrial project is situated in the heart of the largest and most intact forest in the world, Canada's boreal forest. It stretches across northern Canada, including Labrador, where it's home to the largest remaining wild caribou herd in the world: the George River caribou herd, numbering approximately 400,000 animals. Unfortunately, when I was there, I couldn't find one of them, but you have the antlers as proof. All across the boreal, we're blessed with this incredible abundance of wetlands. Wetlands, globally, are among the most endangered ecosystems. They're absolutely critical, as they clean air, clean water, sequester large amounts of greenhouse gases, and are home to a huge diversity of species.

Extreme mass ratio inspirals (EMRIs) can be categorized into dry EMRIs and wet EMRIs based on their formation mechanisms. Dry (or "loss-cone") EMRIs, previously regarded as the primary EMRI sources for the Laser Interferometer Space Antenna, are mainly generated through multi-body scattering in nuclear star clusters and gravitational capture. In this letter, we emphasize an alternative EMRI formation pathway: wet EMRIs facilitated by the accretion flow surrounding accreting galactic-center massive black holes (MBHs). Within this channel, the accretion disk captures stellar-mass black holes initially on inclined orbits, then induces their migration toward the MBH—significantly enhancing the EMRI formation rate in such galaxies by orders of magnitude.

Reducing the legal drink-driving limit could save more lives than previously estimated, according to a new report. Research from University College London suggests that lowering the limit from 80mg to 50mg of alcohol per 100ml of blood could prevent 65 deaths annually. These findings are being released by the Parliamentary Advisory Council for Transport Safety as MPs prepare to vote on the government's road safety bill, which addresses speeding fines but not the drink-driving limit. Conducted by transport expert Professor Richard Allsop, the research also indicates that this change could result in 230 fewer serious injuries on UK roads compared to 2003, potentially saving the country £119 million each year. A prior estimate from a 1998 Department of Transport report projected that such a reduction might save 50 lives annually.

Every day, health headlines capture our attention with claims like, "A single vitamin can lower your risk of heart disease," or "Eating chocolate reduces stress among students." We also hear about breakthroughs like, "New drug extends lives of patients with rare diseases." However, these headlines often contradict each other or oversimplify the complex findings of the underlying research. This discrepancy arises because catchy headlines tend to overlook the nuanced and incremental nature of scientific studies. How can you ensure that you’re not misled by sensationalistic headlines? The key is to examine the original research that the headline is based on. We've crafted hypothetical research scenarios to illustrate this point for each of these headlines. Stay tuned for an explanation of the first example, and take a moment to reflect when you see the headline. These scenarios are simplified for clarity.

Imagine if you could plug your brain into a machine that would bring you ultimate pleasure for the rest of your life. If you were given the choice to sign up for that kind of existence, would you? That's the question philosopher Robert Nozick posed through a thought experiment he called the experience machine. The experiment asks us to consider a world in which scientists have developed a machine that would simulate real life while guaranteeing experiences of only pleasure and never pain. The catch? You have to permanently leave reality behind, but you'll hardly know the difference. Your experiences will be indistinguishable from reality. Life's natural ups and downs will just be replaced with an endless series of ups. Sounds great, right?

Deep hashing methods have demonstrated exceptional retrieval accuracy and efficiency in large-scale image retrieval. The primary focus in these methods is on optimizing discrete hash bits. A common strategy in these methods is to adopt an activation function, for instance, the sigmoid or tanh, and minimize a quantization loss to approximate discrete values. However, this paradigm may lead to more and more hash bits being stuck in the wrong region of the activation functions, failing to escape. We refer to this issue as the 'dead bits problem'. Moreover, the existing quantization loss will exacerbate dbp. In this paper, we propose a simple yet effective gradient amplifier that acts before activation functions to mitigate dbp.

Difference-in-differences analysis with a control group that significantly diverges from a treated group is susceptible to bias from historical events that have disparate effects on the groups. Creating a more closely matched control group by matching a subset of the overall control group to the treated group can potentially mitigate this bias. We investigate this phenomenon through simulation studies. We examine the impact of mountaintop removal mining (mrm) on mortality using a difference-in-differences analysis that leverages the increase in mrm following the 1990 Clean Air Act amendments. For a difference-in-differences analysis of the effect of mrm on mortality, we constructed a more closely matched control group and obtained a 95% confidence interval that encompasses substantial adverse effects, as well as no effect and small beneficial effects.

A Georgia woman has filed a class-action lawsuit against Hewlett-Packard (HP), alleging that the company deliberately programs its printer ink cartridges to deactivate prematurely. According to the complaint, an embedded microchip falsely signals that the cartridge is empty, forcing users to replace it even when ink remains. The lawsuit aims to include all U.S. consumers who purchased HP inkjet printers since February 2001.  

HP, the global leader in printer manufacturing, has declined to address the allegations. While the company states that its cartridge chips are designed to monitor ink levels and prompt timely replacements, the lawsuit contends that these chips are also programmed to disable cartridges on a preset expiration date, irrespective of actual ink supply.

El Paso Natural Gas Pipeline to Conduct Open Season for Mainline Expansion
El Paso Natural Gas, a subsidiary of Kinder Morgan, Inc., will conduct an open season for its Mainline Expansion project to test market interest in additional firm transportation capacity. The pipeline has announced that it will offer up to 14.4 billion cubic feet per day (Bcf/d) of firm transportation capacity for a 10-year term, subject to certain conditions.
The open season is scheduled to run from February 1 to March 10, and interested shippers are invited to submit bids for the available capacity. The pipeline has advised that it will evaluate bids based on a variety of factors, including the shipper's transportation requirements, the distance of the gas to the pipeline, and the shipper's creditworthiness.
The Mainline Expansion project is designed to relieve the apparent capacity constraint at the California border, where El Paso Natural Gas has been under significant pressure from regulators and shippers to expand due to high demand and skyrocketing prices in California. In a January 3 letter to the pipeline, Daniel M. Adamson, Director of FERC's Office of Energy Projects, urged El Paso to consider modifying its Line 2000 project

A recent study inspired by Monte Carlo experiments (Jiménez, Journal of Turbulence, 2020) reveals that dipoles play a crucial role in the dynamics of decaying two-dimensional turbulence, rivaling the importance of individual vortex cores. Notably, the kinetic energy of this flow is predominantly carried by elongated streams formed through the concatenation of dipoles. The vortices in this system undergo a bifurcation, separating into two distinct families: small, rapidly moving cores and larger, slowly moving ones that become 'frozen' into a slowly evolving, crystalline structure. The kinematic properties of these two families exhibit stark differences, with only the smaller, faster-moving vortices displaying self-similarity. Conversely, the larger, slower-moving vortices are responsible for the majority of the flow's kinetic energy and are the primary constituents of the dipoles and streams. The underlying mechanisms driving the growth of this slow-moving component are explored in detail.
