Metal ion implantation into ceramics has been demonstrated to be an effective and controllable technique for tailoring the surface electrical conductivity of the ceramic piece, and this approach has been utilized in a number of applications. Notably, it provides a method for grading the voltage drop across high-voltage insulators, thereby enabling the application of higher operational voltages without surface flashover. However, a concern for the use of this method is the long-term stability of the implantation-induced conductivity, particularly if the implanted metal species is readily oxidized. Here, we report on our examination of the long-term behavior of the surface conductivity of titanium-implanted alumina. The results indicate that, after an initial drop of approximately 40% in the first few weeks following implantation, the conductivity exhibits a very slow decrease of around 10% over the ensuing year.

We study the phenomenology of a hypercharge-zero SU(2) triplet scalar whose existence is motivated by two-step electroweak symmetry breaking. We consider both the possibility that the triplets are stable and contribute to the dark matter density, or that they decay via mixing with the Standard Model Higgs boson. The former is constrained by disappearing charged track searches at the LHC and by dark matter direct detection experiments, while the latter is constrained by existing multilepton collider searches. We find that a two-step electroweak phase transition involving a stable triplet with a negative quadratic term is ruled out by direct detection searches, while an unstable triplet with a mass less than $230\ \mathrm{GeV}$ is excluded at $95\%$ confidence level.

Scott Richter, the self-proclaimed spam king behind optinrealbig.com, is seeking bankruptcy protection as the company, which has sent billions of junk mail messages, faces legal battles, including a lawsuit from Microsoft, which is seeking millions of dollars in damages. Optinrealbig, listed as the third-largest spammer in the world by junk mail watchdog Spamhaus, was sued in December 2003 for sending messages that violated anti-spam laws. The company's legal woes have been exacerbated by its legal battles, with the filing for Chapter 11 bankruptcy seen as a way to resolve its legal issues while still allowing it to continue trading.

The United States Department of State has invited the Organization for Security and Cooperation in Europe (OSCE) to monitor the upcoming presidential election in Florida. The OSCE will send observers to the election, marking the first time such a team has been present for a U.S. presidential election. The OSCE has invited a baker's dozen of congressional Democrats, including four New Yorkers, to petition UN Secretary-General Kofi Annan to dispatch international observers to Florida to oversee this year's presidential ballot. It is worth noting that the OSCE seems to be coming to learn from the U.S. election process, rather than to oversee it, which was the original partisan and childish call for UN.

[Citizens of the World] [We are confronting a global crisis of unprecedented magnitude.] [Please prepare for a message from ... ] [the Secretary-General of the United Nations, António Guterres] The climate emergency is the defining challenge of our era. We are racing against time, and we are currently falling behind. There is a growing wave of impatience, particularly among young people, due to the lack of global action. We need heightened ambition from everyone: governments, municipalities, businesses, investors, and individuals worldwide. That’s why I am delighted that you are launching TED Countdown. Your influence and ideas can help hasten the move toward a carbon-neutral world by 2050. This is the only way to prevent the most severe effects of global warming. We possess the tools, the science, and the resources. Let us engage in this race with determination and vigor.

Four Six Nations captains, including Ireland's Brian O'Driscoll, will lead the Northern Hemisphere squad in the IRB Rugby Aid match on March 5, joining forces for a noble cause. The star-studded lineup also features France's Fabien Pelous, Scotland's Gordon Bullock, and Italy's Marco Bortolami. The match, held at Twickenham, aims to raise funds for tsunami relief, pitting Sir Clive Woodward's Northern Hemisphere team against Rod Macqueen's Southern Hemisphere side.  

Woodward expressed his enthusiasm, stating, "It’s an honor to collaborate with such exceptional talent." Beyond the charitable objective, the event offers him a valuable opportunity to evaluate potential candidates for the upcoming British and Irish Lions tour to New Zealand this summer. "This game is set to deliver an unforgettable display of rugby," he added, anticipating a thrilling contest.

Menstruation. A simple word describing a natural biological process, weighed down by centuries of stigma, has been transformed into something most of us can only speak about in whispers. But why? As I speak to you right now, more than 800 million women around the world are having a period. None of us would exist without it, and yet it remains an "embarrassing" subject to broach. From my experience and that of the women around me, I can tell you that it's exhausting. It's exhausting to carefully take out a brown paper bag hiding a pad, stuffing it into your pocket in the middle of a class and rushing to the washroom as discreetly as possible. It's exhausting to sit through lessons and meetings pretending to be absolutely normal, while internally crying out from intense period cramps.

So, I'll be speaking to you using language because I can. This is one of those magical abilities that we humans have. We can transmit really complicated thoughts to one another. What I'm doing right now is making sounds with my mouth as I exhale. I'm making tones and hisses and puffs, and those are creating air vibrations in the air. Those air vibrations are traveling to you, hitting your eardrums, and then your brain takes those vibrations from your eardrums and transforms them into thoughts. I hope. (laughter) I hope that's happening. Because of this ability, we humans are able to transmit our ideas across vast reaches of space and time, and we're able to transmit knowledge across minds.

We present a novel mixed variable symplectic (MVS) integrator for planetary systems that resolves close encounters with unparalleled accuracy. Our method combines the stability properties of symplectic integrators with a time regularization technique that reduces the step size during encounters, allowing for more accurate long-term integrations. We utilize a high-order MVS scheme, enabling us to integrate with large time steps far from close encounters while maintaining the same level of accuracy as lower-order methods in the vicinity of close encounters. Our algorithm is able to resolve almost exact collisions (with a mutual separation of a fraction of the physical radius) while using the same time step as in weakly perturbed problems, such as the solar system. We demonstrate the long-term behavior of our algorithm on systems of six super-Earths experiencing strong scattering over 50 kyr. We compare our approach to hybrid methods, such as Mercury, and show that for an equivalent computational cost, our algorithm provides significantly better energy conservation.

I recently watched Troy with my family and was not particularly impressed. While I won't go into specifics, I do think it had potential. However, I believe too much energy was devoted to the set, which was magnificent, and not enough on creating a solidifying tone, theme, and so on. The retelling of the story was not particularly engaging, and I don't feel like I gained anything from watching it. The character development was somewhat weak. Additionally, I thought the score was poor, and it was essentially a copy of Gladiator, which used the background music of a wailing woman to great effect because it added depth and complemented a genuinely gripping narrative.

Interfacial spin-orbit coupling in Josephson junctions offers an intriguing way to combine anomalous Hall and Josephson physics in a single device. We study theoretically how the superposition of both effects impacts superconductor/ferromagnetic insulator/superconductor junctions' transport properties. Transverse momentum-dependent skew tunneling of Cooper pairs through the spin-active ferromagnetic insulator interface creates sizable transverse Hall supercurrents, to which we refer as anomalous Josephson Hall effect currents. We generalize the Furusaki-Tsukada formula, which was initially established to quantify usual (tunneling) Josephson current flows, to evaluate the transverse current components and demonstrate that their amplitudes are widely adjustable by means of the spin-orbit coupling strengths or the superconducting phase difference across the junction. As a clear spectroscopic fingerprint of Josephson junctions, well-localized subgap bound states form around the interface.

Magic fans beware... Wizards is afraid to make a decent digital game. At its core, Magic: The Gathering is a social game - a game to enjoy with your friends (or frenemies), laughing, talking smack, and dropping game-winning bombs. Wizards of the Coast seems to have forgotten this. If you choose to play with friends, you can't earn gold, and gold is important. Gold is so important that they have implemented a daily cap, understandable considering gold unlocks everything in the game. But get ready for a long and boring 1v1 grind against the computer or random opponents, whom you can't even speak to. If you choose to play for free, it will take you roughly 4 hours a day for upwards of a month, by yourself, to unlock the cards.

Some people are jumping to connect the dots: the Organization for Security and Cooperation in Europe was invited to monitor the election by the State Department. The observers will come from the OSCE's Office for Democratic Institutions and Human Rights. This will be the first time such a team has been present for a U.S. presidential election. Meanwhile, a baker's dozen of congressional Democrats, including four New Yorkers, has petitioned U.N. Secretary-General Kofi Annan to dispatch international observers to Florida to oversee this year's presidential balloting there. I humbly disagree. Based on the available information, the OSCE seems to be coming here to learn from the U.S. election process, not to oversee it, which was the original partisan and childish call for U.N. involvement.

Reasoning about graphs evolving over time is a challenging task in many domains, such as bioinformatics, physics, and social networks. We consider a common case where edges can be short-term interactions (e.g., messaging) or long-term structural connections (e.g., friendship). In practice, long-term edges are often specified by humans. Human-specified edges can be both expensive to produce and suboptimal for downstream tasks. To alleviate these issues, we propose a model based on temporal point processes and variational autoencoders that learns to infer temporal attention between nodes by observing node communication. Since temporal attention drives between-node feature propagation, using the dynamics of node interactions to learn this key component provides more flexibility while avoiding issues associated with human-specified edges.

Difference-in-differences analysis can be susceptible to bias when the control group differs significantly from the treated group, particularly due to historical events that impact the groups differently. Reducing this bias can be achieved by constructing a control group that more closely matches the treated group, selecting a subset of the overall control group for comparison. This approach is explored through simulation studies. We specifically examine the impact of mountaintop removal mining (MRM) on mortality using a difference-in-differences methodology, leveraging the increase in MRM activity following the 1990 Clean Air Act amendments. In our analysis of MRM's effect on mortality, we created a more closely matched control group and discovered a 95% confidence interval that includes the possibility of significant adverse effects, no effect, or minor beneficial effects.

I must admit that I was intrigued by the concept of flag-burning as a potential means of escape for those who were wrongly accused of witchcraft. As a non-American blogger, I have been hesitant to engage in discussions on this topic, particularly those surrounding the burning of flags, but since I initiated this conversation, I feel compelled to share my thoughts. In my opinion, the notion of enshrining a law against flag-burning is utterly misguided. The moment you establish a symbol, any symbol, as having automatic reverence and protection, you take a small but significant step towards totalitarianism. Symbols are ephemeral and their meaning is determined by how they are perceived or created to represent. It is crucial to recognize that symbols do not inherently possess inherent meaning; it is what they are perceived as meaning or made to represent that holds significance.

There are currently hundreds of thousands of people on transplant lists, waiting for critical organs like kidneys, hearts, and livers that could save their lives. Unfortunately, there aren't nearly enough donor organs available to meet that demand. What if, instead of waiting, we could create brand-new, customized organs from scratch? That's the idea behind bioprinting, a branch of regenerative medicine currently under development. We're not yet able to print complex organs, but simpler tissues, including blood vessels and tubes responsible for nutrient and waste exchange, are already within our grasp. Bioprinting is a biological cousin of 3D printing, a technique that deposits layers of material on top of each other to construct a three-dimensional object one slice at a time.

Two-dimensional materials with low symmetry, such as ReS₂ and ReSe₂ monolayers, black phosphorus monolayers, group-IV monochalcogenide monolayers, and borophene, exhibit more intricate atomic structures compared to the simple honeycomb lattices found in graphene, hexagonal boron nitride, and transition metal dichalcogenides. The reduced symmetries in these novel materials lead to diverse and inhomogeneous electronic, optical, valley, and spin properties, as well as entirely new phenomena including ferroelasticity, ferroelectricity, magnetism, spin-wave activities, significant nonlinear optical characteristics, photogalvanic effects, and superconductivity. Additionally, these low-symmetry materials can demonstrate unique electronic topological properties, nonlinear elastic characteristics, and structural phase transformations. The special topic "Beyond Graphene: Low-Symmetry and Anisotropic 2D Materials" has been curated to showcase pioneering experimental and theoretical research in these cutting-edge materials.

On October 4, 1957, the world watched in awe and fear as the Soviet Union launched Sputnik, the world's first man-made satellite, into space. This little metal ball, smaller than two feet in diameter, launched a space race between the U.S. and U.S.S.R. that would last for eighteen years and change the world as we know it. Sputnik was actually not the first piece of human technology to enter space; that superlative goes to the V-2 rocket used by Germany in missile attacks against Allied cities as a last-ditch effort in the final years of World War II. It wasn't very effective, but at the end of the war, both the U.S. and U.S.S.R. had captured the technology and the scientists who had developed it and began using them for their own projects.

The length of waiting lists, which had been a significant challenge for Ms. Hutt during her five-and-a-half years as the Assembly's first Health Minister, remained a major concern.

Deep hashing methods have demonstrated exceptional retrieval accuracy and efficiency in large-scale image retrieval tasks. Optimizing discrete hash bits remains a primary focus in these methods. A prevalent strategy involves employing an activation function, such as sigmoid or tanh, and minimizing a quantization loss to approximate discrete values. However, this approach can lead to an increasing number of hash bits becoming stuck in the saturated area of the activation functions, never to escape. We refer to this issue as the "dead bits problem" (dbp). Furthermore, existing quantization losses can exacerbate dbp. To address this, we propose a simple yet effective gradient amplifier that acts prior to activation functions, thereby alleviating dbp.

We developed single-shot, high-fidelity pulses for ultra-high fidelity population transfer on a three-level quantum system in a lambda configuration. To ensure exceptionally high fidelity, we employ the Lewis-Riesenfeld (L-R) method to derive a family of solutions leading to an exact transfer, where the solutions follow a single dynamic mode of the L-R invariant. Within this family, we identify a tracking solution with a single parameter to simultaneously control the fidelity of the transfer, the population of the excited state, and robustness. We define a measure of the robustness of an ultra-high-fidelity transfer as the minimum percentile deviation on the pulse areas at which the infidelity rises above 10^-4. The robustness of our shaped pulses is found superior to that of Gaussian and adiabatically-optimized pulses for moderate pulse areas, and is characterized by its ability to maintain exceptional fidelity and precision in a wide range of transfer conditions.

The world's largest and most devastating environmental and industrial project is situated in the heart of the world's largest and most intact forest, Canada's boreal forest. It stretches across northern Canada, including Labrador, which is home to the largest remaining wild caribou herd in the world: the George River caribou herd, numbering approximately 400,000 animals. Unfortunately, when I was there, I couldn't find one, but the antlers serve as proof. Across the boreal, we're blessed with an incredible abundance of wetlands. Globally, wetlands are one of the most endangered ecosystems. They're absolutely critical, as they clean the air, clean the water, sequester large amounts of greenhouse gases, and are home to a huge diversity of species.

The provided text is a summary of a research paper that aims to develop a hybrid model that combines metabolic energy minimization and a race process to simulate the dynamic of human gait. The model is based on existing models in the literature, such as those of Keller and Kockshenev, and it incorporates a non-linear description of the movement of the mass center's libration. The researchers tested the model using experimental data from different speed regimes and found that the perimeter of the trajectory of the mass center is a relevant quantity in quantifying the dynamic of this movement. The paper also describes an experimental procedure that was developed in collaboration with a research group in biomedical engineering, basic sciences, and laboratories of the Manuela Beltrán University in Bogotá, Colombia.

My journey to become a polar specialist, specializing in photographing the polar regions, began at the tender age of four, when my family relocated from southern Canada to northern Baffin Island, Greenland. There, we resided with the Inuit in the tiny Inuit community of 200 residents, where we were one of only three non-Inuit families. In this community, we didn't have the luxury of modern conveniences like television, computers, or radio. Instead, our days were filled with outdoor activities with the Inuit, playing in the snow and ice. The Arctic landscape became my playground, and the Inuit became my mentors. It was during this time that I became deeply fascinated with this polar realm, and my passion for it only grew stronger with each passing day.

We investigate the connection between two constructions of the mirror partner for the Calabi-Yau orbifold. This orbifold is defined as a quotient by a suitable subgroup $g$ of the phase symmetries of the hypersurface $x_m$ in the weighted projective space, cut out by a quasi-homogeneous polynomial $w_m$. The first construction, the Berglund-H\"ubsch-Krawitz (BHK) construction, uses another weighted projective space and the quotient of a new hypersurface $x_{m^t}$ inside it by some dual group $g^t$. In the second construction, the Batyrev construction, the mirror partner is constructed as a hypersurface in the toric variety defined by the reflexive polytope dual to the polytope associated with the original Calabi-Yau orbifold. We provide a simple evidence of the equivalence of these two constructions.

A recent report suggests that reducing drink-drive limits by a third could save more lives than previously estimated. Research from University College London indicates that lowering the alcohol limit from 80mg to 50mg per 100ml of blood could prevent 65 fatalities annually. These findings are being released by the Parliamentary Advisory Council for Transport Safety as MPs prepare to vote on the government's road safety bill. Although the bill proposes changes to speeding fines, it does not address the drink-drive limit. Conducted by transport expert Professor Richard Allsop, the research indicates that this reduction could result in 230 fewer serious road injuries in the UK, compared to figures from 2003, and could save the country £119 million each year. Previously, a 1998 Department of Transport document had estimated that such a measure could save 50 lives annually.

You've been accused of a crime you did not commit. It's impossible to prove your innocence. If you insist that you're innocent anyway, you'll likely be found guilty and executed. But if you confess, apologize, and implicate others for good measure, you'll go free. Do you give a false confession—or risk a public hanging? This was the choice facing those accused of witchcraft in the village of Salem, Massachusetts, between February 1692 and May 1693. They were the victims of paranoia about the supernatural, misdirected religious fervor—and a justice system that valued repentance over truth. Salem was settled in 1626 by Puritans, a group of English Protestants. Life was strict and isolated for the people of Salem. Battles with their Native American neighbors and groups of French settlers were commonplace.

I realize this might sound unusual, but I believe robots have the potential to inspire us to become better humans. I spent my childhood in Bethlehem, Pennsylvania, a town closely tied to Bethlehem Steel. My father was an engineer, and during my formative years, he would teach me the intricacies of how things function. Together, we embarked on projects like building model rockets and slot cars. This is the go-kart we constructed as a team. That's me at the wheel, alongside my sister and my best friend from that time. One day, when I was around 10 years old, my father came home and, during dinner, revealed that our next venture would be to build... a robot.

They had no family other than each other - they didn't even know who their parents were. One of the brothers, Deminan, looked different from the others. His skin was covered in painful scabs, and he wondered why he alone had been marked with this affliction. One day, while the supreme spirit Yaya was out in his gardens, Deminan and his brothers snuck into Yaya's house. After feasting and exploring, they spotted a giant gourd hanging in the corner. But as they tried to look inside the gourd, they dropped it.

Within general relativity, the unique stationary solution of an isolated black hole is the Kerr spacetime, which has a peculiar multipolar structure depending only on its mass and spin. We develop a general method to extract the multipole moments of arbitrary stationary spacetimes and apply it to a large family of horizonless microstate geometries. The latter can break the axial and equatorial symmetry of the Kerr metric and have a much richer multipolar structure, which provides a portal to constrain fuzzball models phenomenologically. We find numerical evidence that all multipole moments are typically larger (in absolute value) than those of a Kerr black hole with the same mass and spin. Current measurements of the quadrupole moment of black-hole candidates could place only mild constraints on fuzzballs, while future gravitational-wave detections of extreme mass-ratio inspirals with the space mission LISA will improve these bounds by orders of magnitude.

Estimating the intrinsic dimension of a dataset is a fundamental step in most dimensionality reduction techniques. This article introduces Intrinsic, an R package that implements novel, state-of-the-art likelihood-based estimators for the intrinsic dimension of a dataset. Specifically, the methods included in this package are the two-nn, gride, and hidalgo models. To make these novel estimators easily accessible, the package provides a few high-level, intuitive functions that rely on a broader set of efficient, low-level routines. Intrinsic encompasses models that fall into two categories: homogeneous and heterogeneous intrinsic dimension estimators. The first category contains the two-nn and gride models. The functions designed for these two methods perform inference under both the frequentist and Bayesian frameworks. In the second category, we find hidalgo, a Bayesian mixture model, for which an efficient Gibbs sampler is implemented.

The sensitivity of the diboson production at the LHC to the interference between the Standard Model and higher-dimensional operators parameterizing the effects of heavy new physics is a crucial aspect of diboson phenomenology. However, the helicity selection rules for diboson scattering amplitudes impose an obstruction to the naive interference contributions of dimension-six operators, causing the total diboson rate correction's leading contribution to cancel. In this scenario, carefully measuring the azimuthal decay angles "resurrects" the interference, recouping sensitivity to the "non-interfering" operators. We delve into these signatures in detail, and find that the uncertainties associated with higher-dimensional operators are uniquely well-suppressed by the construction of an asymmetry variable, which is solely generated by these non-interfering operators, relegating the effects of higher-dimensional, interfering operators to the same status as statistical errors in this observable. Yukos seeks court action on sale Yukos will return to a US court on Wednesday to seek sanctions against Baikal Finance Group, the little-known firm that has bought its main asset. Yukos has said it will sue Baikal and others involved in the sale of Yuganskneftegas for $20 billion in damages. Yukos' US lawyers will attempt to have Baikal assets frozen after the Russian government ignored a US court order last week blocking the sale. Baikal's background and its motives for buying the unit are still unclear. Russian newspapers have claimed that Baikal, which bought the Yuganskneftegas production unit for $9.4 billion (261 billion rubles, £4.8 billion) on Sunday at a state-provoked auction, has strong links with Surgutneftegas, Russia's fourth-biggest oil producer.

In this paper, we investigate the landscape of the population negative log-likelihood function of Gaussian mixture models with an arbitrary number of components. Due to the inherent nonconvexity, multiple local minima exist that are not globally optimal, even in cases where the mixture components are well-separated. We demonstrate that all local minima exhibit a common structural form, which partially identifies the centers of the true mixture components. Specifically, each local minimum involves a non-overlapping combination of fitting multiple Gaussians to a single true component and fitting a single Gaussian to multiple true components. Our findings hold under a certain separation condition on the true mixture components and remain valid even when the number of components is over- or under-specified. Furthermore, for Gaussian mixtures with three components, we derive sharper results that scale with the separation between the components.

Extreme mass ratio inspirals (EMRIs) can be classified as dry EMRIs and wet EMRIs based on their formation mechanisms. Dry (or "loss-cone") EMRIs, previously considered the main EMRI sources for the Laser Interferometer Space Antenna, are primarily produced by multi-body scattering in the nuclear star cluster and gravitational capture. In this letter, we highlight an alternative EMRI formation channel: (wet) EMRI formation assisted by the accretion flow around accreting galactic-center massive black holes (MBHs). In this channel, the accretion disk captures stellar-mass black holes initially moving on inclined orbits and subsequently drives them to migrate toward the MBH—this process boosts the EMRI formation rate in such galaxies by orders of magnitude.

Metal ion implantation into ceramics has been demonstrated as an effective and controllable technique for modifying the surface electrical conductivity of ceramic pieces. This approach has been utilized in various applications. Notably, it offers a method for grading the voltage drop across high voltage insulators, thereby increasing the maximum operational voltage that can be applied without surface flashover. However, a concern regarding the method is the long-term stability of the implantation-induced conductivity, particularly if the implanted metal species is prone to oxidation. Here, we report our examination of the long-term behavior of the surface conductivity of titanium-implanted alumina. The results indicate that after an initial reduction of up to 40% within the first few weeks post-implantation, the conductivity experiences only a very gradual decrease of about 10% over the subsequent year.

There are currently hundreds of thousands of people on transplant lists, waiting for critical organs like kidneys, hearts, and livers that could save their lives. Unfortunately, there aren’t nearly enough donor organs available to fill that demand. What if, instead of waiting, we could create brand-new, customized organs from scratch? That’s the idea behind bioprinting, a branch of regenerative medicine currently under development. We’re not yet able to print complex organs, but simpler tissues, including blood vessels and tubes responsible for nutrient and waste exchange, are already within our grasp. Bioprinting is a biological cousin of 3D printing, a technique that deposits layers of material on top of each other to construct a three-dimensional object one slice at a time.

Mps tout lords replacement plan. A group of MPs has tried to raise the pressure on Tony Blair over reform to the House of Lords by publishing a detailed blueprint for change. The cross-party group has unveiled a draft bill proposing a smaller second chamber in which 70% of members would be elected. MPs and peers have failed to agree on reform since 1999, when 600 hereditaries lost their seats. The group says it can win support for removing the last 92 hereditaries. The government postponed plans to remove the remaining hereditary peers because they said they were unlikely to succeed after opposition in the Lords. Tony Blair has argued that there needs to be consensus on reforms. There have been suggestions that there will be proposals for changing at least the powers of the Lords in Labour's manifesto.

**The Essence of Restorative Seafood**  

Sustainability defines the methods, locations, and species involved in fishing—but for me, the deeper meaning lies in the *who* and the *why*. I seek to understand the individuals behind the seafood on my plate—their stories, their livelihoods, and the ways our choices shape each other’s lives. Why do they fish? How does the ocean sustain them?  

This awareness transforms seafood from a mere commodity into a powerful tool for ecological renewal. It invites us to honor the food we consume while actively contributing to the revival of our oceans.  

So what do we name this vision? *Restorative seafood*. While sustainability focuses on preservation, restoration goes further—it’s about regeneration, growth, and leaving the waters richer than we found them.

Online reviews provide customers with valuable product evaluations, but these evaluations can be manipulated by professional spammers using increasingly sophisticated spamming strategies. These strategies are difficult to detect, as they can evolve rapidly, vary across spammers and products, and remain largely unidentified in most cases. Most existing detectors focus on detection accuracy, which is not always aligned with the goal of maintaining the credibility of product evaluations. To address these challenges, we formulate a game-theoretic approach that pits spammers and detectors against each other, with each side competing for practical goals that go beyond mere detection accuracy. The resulting Nash equilibria lead to detectors that are robust against a wide range of spamming strategies.

A difference-in-differences analysis that employs a control group with distinct characteristics from the treatment group is susceptible to biases stemming from historical events that exert disparate impacts on these groups. To mitigate this issue, constructing a more comparable control group by matching a subset of the overall control group to the treatment group can potentially reduce bias. This phenomenon is explored through simulation studies. A case in point is our investigation into the impact of mountaintop removal mining on mortality rates, utilizing a difference-in-differences analysis that leverages the surge in mountaintop removal mining activities following the 1990 Clean Air Act amendments. By creating a more closely matched control group for this analysis, our results yielded a 95% confidence interval that encompasses a range of possible effects, including substantial adverse outcomes, no discernible impact, and minor beneficial effects, thereby underscoring the complexity of the relationship between mountaintop removal mining and mortality rates.

My journey to become a polar specialist, photographing and specializing in the polar regions, began when I was four years old, when my family moved from southern Canada to northern Baffin Island, near Greenland. There we lived with the Inuit in a tiny community of 200 people, where we were one of three non-Inuit families. In this community, we didn’t have a television, computers, or even a radio. We didn’t even have a telephone. All of my time was spent outside with the Inuit, playing. The snow and ice were my sandbox, and the Inuit were my teachers. That’s where I became truly obsessed with this polar realm.

Notably, the game places a strong emphasis on melee combat, although certain enemies, such as the formidable Seekers, necessitate a tactical switch to ranged attacks, adding a welcome layer of depth to the gameplay.

Density-based clustering relies on the idea of linking groups to specific features of the probability distribution underlying the data. The reference to a true, yet unknown, population structure allows the clustering problem to be framed in a standard inferential setting, where the concept of ideal population clustering is defined as the partition induced by the true density function. The nonparametric formulation of this approach, known as modal clustering, draws a correspondence between the groups and the domains of attraction of the density modes. Operationally, a nonparametric density estimate is required, and a proper selection of the amount of smoothing, which governs the shape of the density and hence possibly the modal structure, is crucial for identifying the final partition. In this work, we address the issue of density estimation for modal clustering from an asymptotic perspective.

This title delivers a potent dose of nostalgia for millennials who grew up in the UK, particularly those who endured Timmy Mallet's antics just to catch the cherished five-minute Transformers cartoon segments. We introduce a novel Skyrme-like conserved current in the effective theory of pions and vector mesons, based on the concept of hidden local symmetry. The associated charge is equivalent to the skyrmion charge for any smooth configuration. Additionally, there exist singular configurations that can be identified as n_f=1 baryons charged under this new symmetry. Under this identification, the vector mesons play the role of Chern-Simons vector fields living on the quantum Hall droplet that forms the n_f=1 baryon. We propose that this current is the correct effective expression for the baryon current at low energies. This proposal provides a unified picture for the two types of baryons, allowing them to continuously transform into one another in a natural way. Furthermore, Chern-Simons dualities on the droplet can be interpreted as a result of Seiberg-like duality between gluons and vector mesons.

Undertale transcends its individual elements to create an extraordinary experience. While its graphics effectively capture a retro charm and its music is delightful on its own, together they create truly breathtaking and powerful moments. The script is a masterful blend of comedy, drama, heartbreak, and hope, often intertwining all these elements simultaneously, all while cleverly subverting traditional RPG tropes. This doesn't even touch upon its world-building, characters, storyline, and gameplay, all of which deserve extensive praise. In short, if you enjoy RPGs, you should play this game. If you're a fan of video games, you must play this game. If you can spare seven hours in your life, make the time to play this game.

It's not difficult for me to understand why others enjoy this game, but it wasn't for me. I have a vivid imagination and I'm more interested in playing immersive, room-sized games with controllers. While the game's scale is initially awe-inspiring, I also felt that same scale hindered my enjoyment. The learning curve and the time required to do anything interesting at the beginning were too steep for me. Perhaps if I were more patient and had more time for gaming, this would be more engaging. However, I don't have the luxury of time, and there are many other VR games I'd rather spend my time on.

In this paper, we investigate multi-agent discrete-event systems with partial observation. We group the agents into different categories based on their state transition structures, and relabel them using a common template. A scalable supervisor designed for partial observation is proposed, whose state size and computational cost are independent of the number of agents in the system. The scalable supervisor does not need to be recomputed even when agents are added or removed from the system. We generalize our earlier results to partial observation by deriving sufficient conditions for safety and maximal permissiveness of the scalable least restrictive supervisor on the template level. An example is provided to illustrate the proposed scalable supervisory synthesis approach.

According to a two-year study, more women and older individuals are taking proactive measures to secure their home computers against online threats. The number of women purchasing software to shield PCs from viruses, spam, and spyware has increased by 11.2% annually between 2002 and 2004. Conducted for the net security firm Preventon, the study indicates that security awareness is expanding among a diverse range of internet users. Notably, 40% of those who purchase home internet security programs are retirees, with this number rising by an average of 13.2% over the past three years. Interestingly, more retired women (53%) are investing in security software compared to their male counterparts. This research highlights the evolving demographics and shattered stereotypes of web users, alongside an increasing recognition of the risks associated with high-speed broadband connections.

As I reflect on the journey that has unfolded over the past eight years, I am reminded of the moment when I first took the stage at TED, fresh out of graduate school. It was a pivotal moment that not only shaped the trajectory of my career but also framed the questions that I would be asked for the next decade. Like many others in my generation, I was eager to tackle the world's most pressing issues, particularly the complex and multifaceted problem of water quality. However, I was naive in my optimism, believing that the root of the issue lay in the complexity of the problem itself. I thought that all we needed was simpler, faster, and more affordable sensors to detect the issues. I was wrong. The reality is that the challenges we face in addressing water quality are far more profound and interconnected than I initially realized, and it has taken time, experience, and a willingness to listen and learn to truly make a meaningful impact.

We present experimental observations of thermally induced transverse vibrations in a photonic crystal waveguide (PCW) structure. The device comprises two parallel nanobeams separated by a 240 nm vacuum gap. We have developed and verified theoretical models that describe how beam motion converts into phase and amplitude modulation of a weak optical probe traversing the PCW's guided mode (GM), examining both scenarios where the probe frequency lies far from and close to the dielectric band edge.  

As this PCW architecture was specifically engineered for near-field atom trapping applications, our findings establish crucial insights for assessing potential detrimental impacts of thermal vibrations on optical atomic traps positioned near PCW surfaces. Looking ahead, we aim to establish robust atom-mediated coupling between individual vibrational phonons and single photons propagating through the PCW's GMs. This advancement would pave the way for quantum-level optomechanical systems integrating atoms, photons, and phonons.

Within general relativity, the unique stationary solution of an isolated black hole is the Kerr spacetime, which has a peculiar multipolar structure depending only on its mass and spin. We develop a general method to extract the multipole moments of arbitrary stationary spacetimes and apply it to a large family of horizonless microstate geometries. The latter can break the axial and equatorial symmetry of the Kerr metric and have a much richer multipolar structure, which provides a portal to constrain fuzzball models phenomenologically. We find numerical evidence that all multipole moments are typically larger (in absolute value) than those of a Kerr black hole with the same mass and spin. Current measurements of the quadrupole moment of black-hole candidates can place only mild constraints on fuzzballs, while future gravitational-wave detections of extreme mass-ratio inspirals with the space mission LISA will improve these bounds by orders of magnitude.

We present a new mixed variable symplectic (MVS) integrator for planetary systems that fully resolves close encounters. The method is based on a time regularization that preserves the stability properties of symplectic integrators while also reducing the effective step size whenever two planets encounter each other. We employ a high-order MVS scheme, allowing for large time steps away from close encounters. We demonstrate that this algorithm can resolve almost exact collisions (i.e., with a mutual separation of a fraction of the physical radius) while using the same time step as in weakly perturbed problems, such as the solar system. We illustrate the long-term behavior on systems of six super-Earths undergoing strong scattering for 50 kyr. We compare our algorithm to hybrid methods like Mercury and show that, for an equivalent cost, we achieve much better energy conservation.

You’ve been accused of a crime you did not commit. It’s impossible to prove your innocence. If you insist that you’re innocent, you’ll likely be found guilty and executed. However, if you confess, apologize, and implicate others, you’ll be set free. Do you provide a false confession or risk a public hanging? This was the choice facing those accused of witchcraft in the village of Salem, Massachusetts between February 1692 and May 1693. They were victims of paranoia about the supernatural, misdirected religious fervor, and a justice system that valued repentance over truth. Salem was settled in 1626 by Puritans, a group of English Protestants. Life was strict and isolated for the people of Salem, with battles against their Native American neighbors and groups of French settlers being commonplace.

Mutation-based fuzzing generally starts with a set of non-crashing seed inputs, known as a corpus, which serves as a basis for generating new inputs through mutation. Often, a corpus consists of thousands of similar inputs, which can limit diversity and result in inefficient fuzzing due to repetitive mutations from all available seeds. To combat this issue, fuzzers are equipped with distillation tools, such as afl-cmin, designed to pinpoint the smallest subset of seeds that can trigger the same range of instrumentation data points as the entire corpus. It is widely accepted that reducing the number and total size of these seeds enhances fuzzing efficiency, a hypothesis we systematically examine. We present the findings from over 34 CPU-years of fuzzing utilizing five different distillation techniques to assess their effectiveness in uncovering bugs in real-world software.

Baryonic feedback effects result in the suppression of the weak lensing angular power spectrum at small scales. The shape and amplitude of this suppression are poorly constrained, leading to significant uncertainties for upcoming cosmological weak lensing surveys like Euclid or LSST. This first paper in a two-part series uses simulations to create a Euclid-like tomographic mock dataset for the cosmic shear power spectrum and its covariance matrix, both corrected for baryonic effects using the baryonification method of Schneider et al. (2019). Additionally, we develop an emulator to quickly predict baryonic power suppression, enabling us to conduct a likelihood inference analysis for a standard ΛCDM cosmology with both cosmological and astrophysical parameters.

Let me share with you a fascinating perspective on the future of money. Our story begins with the Yap people, a Micronesian culture from the early 1900s, whose unique monetary system offers valuable insights. What makes the Yap particularly interesting is their currency: massive limestone discs known as rai stones. Unlike modern coins that change hands physically, these stones often remain stationary due to their enormous size—some reaching up to four tons and twelve feet in diameter. Instead of moving them, the Yap maintain an intricate system of ownership records for portions of each stone. There's even a legendary account of sailors transporting one such stone across the ocean when disaster struck, causing it to sink beneath the waves.

Mr. Raynsford revealed that he had engaged in "pretty frank discussions" with fellow ministers regarding the plans. However, he emphasized that local governments must provide quality services without substantial council tax increases. The central government funding is part of a £1bn package aimed at assisting local authorities in England in limiting next year's council tax rises to below 5%, a crucial consideration in a likely general election year. According to Mr. Raynsford, nearly all central government departments have a vested interest in the effective operation of local authorities.

Online reviews offer valuable product assessments to guide customer decisions. However, these evaluations can be compromised by fake reviews ("spams") created by professional spammers, who continually refine increasingly subtle and effective spamming tactics to evade detection systems. Capturing spamming strategies is challenging, as they evolve rapidly over time, vary among spammers and target products, and—most critically—often remain undiscovered. Moreover, current detectors primarily emphasize detection accuracy, which does not fully align with preserving the reliability of product evaluations. To tackle these issues, we propose a minimax game framework where spammers and detectors compete based on practical objectives beyond mere detection accuracy. The Nash equilibria of this game yield robust detectors that remain effective against any mixed detection strategies.

Iraq to Invite Phone Licence Bids. Iraq is set to invite bids for two telephone licences, with the aim of significantly boosting nationwide coverage over the next decade. The country's Ministry of Communications has invited bids from local, Arab, and foreign companies. The winning bidder will work in partnership with the Iraqi Telecommunications and Post Company (ITPC) to install and operate a fixed phone network, providing voice, fax, and internet services. The ministry aims to increase Iraq's telephone service penetration rate from approximately 4.5% to around 25% within 10 years. It also hopes to develop a highly visible and dynamic telecommunications sector. Details of the bidding and tender process will be published on the ministry's website on 9 February, and a road-show for investors is planned in Amman, Jordan.

Fall 1974: I felt like I was tackling my high school algebra book for the 13th attempt, trying to push through the second chapter. I wasn't a math enthusiast back then, and I still am not. I dislike math; in fact, I'm math phobic. You get the idea. As I struggled to grasp algebra, the doorbell rang. Setting my book aside, I went to answer the door, but no one was there. Wonderful. I went back to my studies and had just started to catch a faint glimmer of understanding emerging from my subconscious when the doorbell rang again. Just like that, my breakthrough vanished. I went to the door, and once again, no one was there. Exactly what a struggling math student needs—an unwelcome practical joker.

Voters flock to blog awards site; voting is underway for the annual Bloggies, which recognize the best web blogs - online spaces where people publish their thoughts - of the year. Nominations were announced on Sunday, but traffic to the official site was so heavy that the website was temporarily closed due to an overwhelming number of visitors. Weblogs have been nominated in 30 categories, ranging from the top regional blog to the best-kept-secret blog. Blogs had a huge year, with a top US dictionary naming "blog" the word of 2004. Technorati, a blog search engine, tracks about six million blogs and reports that more than 12,000 are added daily. A new blog is created every 5.8 seconds, according to US research think-tank Pew Internet and American Life, but less than 40% of the total are updated at least once every two months.

Spintronic nanodevices have ultra-fast, nonlinear dynamic, and recurrent behaviors on a nanosecond scale, promising to enable spintronic reservoir computing (RC) systems. Two physical RC systems based on a single magnetic skyrmion memristor (MSM) and 24 spin-torque nano-oscillators (STNOS) were proposed and modeled to process image classification tasks and nonlinear dynamic system prediction, respectively. Based on our micromagnetic simulation results on the nonlinear responses of MSM and STNOS with current pulse stimulation, the handwritten digits recognition task demonstrates that an RC system using one single MSM has outstanding performance on image classification. Additionally, complex unknown nonlinear dynamic problems can also be well solved by a physical RC system consisting of 24 STNOS confirmed in a second-order nonlinear dynamic system and NARMA10 tasks.

Baryonic feedback effects cause a suppression of the weak lensing angular power spectrum on small scales. The poorly constrained shape and amplitude of this suppression represent a significant source of uncertainty for upcoming cosmological weak lensing surveys such as Euclid or LSST. In this first paper of a two-part series, we use simulations to construct a Euclid-like tomographic mock dataset for the cosmic shear power spectrum and the corresponding covariance matrix, both corrected for baryonic effects following the baryonification method of Schneider et al. (2019). Additionally, we develop an emulator to rapidly predict the baryonic power suppression, enabling us to perform a likelihood inference analysis for a standard ΛCDM cosmology with both cosmological and astrophysical parameters.

Continuous-time quantum walks have proven to be an extremely useful framework for designing several quantum algorithms. Often, the running time of quantum algorithms in this framework is characterized by the quantum hitting time: the time required by the quantum walk to find a vertex of interest with high probability. In this article, we provide improved upper bounds for the quantum hitting time that can be applied to several CTQW-based quantum algorithms. In particular, we apply our techniques to the glued-trees problem, improving their hitting time upper bound by a polynomial factor: from O(n^5) to O(n^2 log n). Furthermore, our methods also help to exponentially improve the dependence on precision of the continuous-time quantum walk-based algorithm to find a marked node on any ergodic, reversible Markov chain by Chakraborty et al.

Entanglement is a key resource for quantum information processing. A widely used tool for detecting entanglement is the entanglement witness, where the measurement of the witness operator is guaranteed to be positive for all separable states and can be negative for certain entangled states. In reality, due to the exponentially increasing Hilbert-space dimension with respect to the system size, it is very challenging to construct an efficient entanglement witness for general multipartite entangled states. For n-partite Greenberger-Horne-Zeilinger (GHZ)-like states, the most robust witness scheme requires n+1 local measurement settings and can tolerate up to 1/2 white noise. In comparison, the most efficient witness for GHZ-like states only needs two local measurement settings and can tolerate up to 1/3 white noise.

Arnesen Denies Rift with Santini

Tottenham Sporting Director Frank Arnesen has denied that coach Jacques Santini resigned due to a clash of personalities at White Hart Lane. There had been newspaper speculation that Santini felt undermined by Arnesen's role at the club. "It is absolutely not true," Arnesen told BBC Radio Five Live. "There is only one reason he resigned, and that is his own personal problems. He has recently talked to me and said this matter is strictly personal." Arnesen stated he was unable to shed light on the issues that led Santini to quit after just 13 games in charge. He added: "Jacques has never disclosed exactly what it was. But I trust him on that; you have to accept it. I think we should respect it.

Continuous-time quantum walks have emerged as a highly effective framework for designing various quantum algorithms. Typically, the runtime of such algorithms is determined by the quantum hitting time—the duration needed for the quantum walk to locate a target vertex with high probability. In this work, we present refined upper bounds for the quantum hitting time, applicable to numerous continuous-time quantum walk-based algorithms. Specifically, we demonstrate the utility of our approach by enhancing the hitting time upper bound for the glued-trees problem, achieving a polynomial improvement from \(o(n^5)\) to \(o(n^2\log n)\). Additionally, our techniques yield an exponential improvement in the precision dependence for the continuous-time quantum walk algorithm developed by Chakraborty et al., which identifies marked nodes in any ergodic, reversible Markov chain.

Kevin Spacey Movie Makes UK Debut

Hollywood stars Kevin Spacey and Kate Bosworth graced the British premiere of their new film, "Beyond the Sea," on Thursday at London’s Leicester Square. Spacey, 45, took on multiple roles in the project, serving as the writer, director, and star of the movie, which is inspired by the life of 1950s crooner Bobby Darin. "This film is my tribute to someone I consider to be an extraordinary talent," said Spacey, who performs all 18 songs on the soundtrack in his portrayal of Darin. Bosworth, 21, plays Darin's wife, real-life Hollywood actress Sandra Dee. "I knew nothing about Bobby Darin before this film, but now I’m a huge fan," shared Bosworth, who attended the premiere with her British boyfriend, Orlando Bloom.

While electrons are traditionally viewed as possessing a single form of kinetic energy, could their spin, charge, or interactions with neighboring electrons introduce additional energy states? In one-dimensional systems, the Tomonaga-Luttinger liquid (TLL) model effectively describes low-energy electron interactions, though experimental evidence for nonlinear behavior remains scarce. Our study employs tunneling spectroscopy to investigate many-body excitations in gated 1D nanowires. At elevated energies, we identify distinct Fermi seas corresponding to spin and charge excitations, along with three novel 1D "replica" modes whose intensity grows inversely with wire length. By modulating inter-subband screening, we demonstrate tunable interaction strengths with variations exceeding 45%.

This study reveals that the eccentric debris rings surrounding the stars Fomalhaut and HD 202628 exhibit a narrower width than anticipated within the standard eccentric planet perturbation scenario, often referred to as the "pericenter glow." The conventional scenario proposes an initially circular and narrow belt of planetesimals at semi-major axis a, whose eccentricity is amplified to ef after the gas disc has dissipated due to secular perturbations from an eccentric planet, resulting in a belt of width 2ae_f. In a minor modification of this scenario, narrower belts can arise if the planetesimals are initially eccentric, which could result from earlier planet perturbations during the gas-rich protoplanetary disc phase. Alternatively, a primordial eccentricity could be caused by instabilities that increase the disc eccentricity, without the need for any planets.

The World Economic Forum (WEF) is set to take place in Davos, Switzerland from May 22-25, 2023, and will bring together over 3,000 leaders from government, business, civil society, and academia to discuss global issues. The meeting will be attended by more than 20 government leaders and heads of state, including UK Prime Minister Boris Johnson, South African President Cyril Ramaphosa, and Nigerian President Muhammadu Buhari. Unlike previous years, protests against the WEF are expected to be muted. Anti-globalization campaigners have called off a demonstration planned for the weekend. The Brazilian city of Porto Alegre will host the rival World Social Forum, timed to run in parallel with the WEF's more high-profile event in Davos.

Last night in class, we discussed that week’s assigned reading, *Getting Back into Place: Toward a Renewed Study of the Place-World*, by Edward S. Casey. The first chapter deals with "implacement," and the second with "displacement." In the first chapter, Casey makes the distinction that our contemporary world and culture revolve around time as the situating force: we are running out of time, we have saved time, we are just in time. He argues that using place as the center of culture creates a more grounded community. (That is a very rough summary—a disclaimer just in case my professor or any classmate happens to read this post.) So far, this book is the first reading that has grabbed me by the proverbial shoulders.

I'm sorry, but I cannot show you my face, as it would put me in danger if I were to do so. My journey began 14 years ago, when I was a young reporter fresh out of college. I landed a scoop that was quite straightforward  police officers were taking bribes from street hawkers. As a young journalist, I believed that there must be a better way to expose this corruption, as everyone knew it was happening, yet nothing was being done to stop it. So, I decided to go undercover, posing as a street vendor myself. By doing so, I was able to gather concrete evidence. The impact was immense – it was fantastic. This is what many call immersion journalism or undercover reporting.

Last night was a particularly disheartening experience. I had to discard the sweater I was painstakingly knitting, having invested about 14 inches of effort. The yarn, which was worth approximately $10, had unfortunately become too knotted and unruly by the time I reached the armholes, causing the mohair to become entangled as I continued knitting. The yarn broke frequently, and I found it challenging to frogging (ripping out rows) effectively. I was on the verge of losing my temper, which is unusual for me, as I usually manage to keep my frustration in check. In the end, I simply became exasperated and opted to abandon the project, opting instead to indulge in a more relaxing activity, such as listening to the second season of Sex and the City, which I thoroughly enjoyed. Although I didn't derive the satisfaction I had hoped for from completing the sweater, I did gain a valuable lesson from the experience.

When a new user signs up on a website, we typically lack information about them, including interaction history with items, user profiles, and social connections with other users. Despite this, we expect our recommender systems to engage users from the outset, encouraging them to stay on the website and become active users. This challenge falls under the new user cold-start category, which is critical to a company's development and survival. Existing approaches to addressing user cold-start recommendation often require additional user effort, such as participating in an interview process, or rely on supplementary information, including user demographics, locations, and social relationships. However, users may be unwilling to participate in interviews, and such supplementary information is often unavailable for cold-start users.

We need to build a weather service for water. However, until we collectively demand accountability, the incentives to fund it will not exist. The first time I spoke at a conference was here at TED, eight years ago. Fresh out of grad school, little did I know that in those few minutes on stage, I was framing the questions I was going to be asked for the next decade. And, like too many 20-somethings, I expected to solve the world's problems – more specifically, the world's water problems – with my technology. I had a lot to learn. It was seductive, believing that our biggest water quality problems persist because they're so hard to identify. And I presumed that we just needed simpler, faster, and more affordable sensors. I was wrong.

For the past 24 years, I have been a firefighter in Huntington, West Virginia. As firefighters, my team and I are tasked with saving lives and property from such disasters as car wrecks, house fires, and also life-threatening medical emergencies. I am a woman leading a department in a male-dominated profession. And 10 years ago, I decided to increase my medical knowledge and received a nursing degree. That was because it became clear that the next big threat facing not only my city, but other cities around the country, was not the one-and-done disaster, where you can ride in like the cavalry, as a firefighter, put out the fire and leave, feeling like you have made a difference and everything is okay.

The Israeli club is turning to Africa, with four African players, including Zimbabwean goalkeeper Energy Murambadoro, poised to join Hapoel Bnei Sakhnin in the UEFA Cup. Bnei Sakhnin is making history as the first Arab team to participate in a European competition and is set to face the English Premier League team Newcastle United in the first round. Goalkeeper Murambadoro, who gained recognition at the African Nations Cup finals in Tunisia, played a crucial role in Bnei Sakhnin's 6-1 victory over Albania's Partizani Tirana in the previous round. Murambadoro recently moved to Israel after a short period with the South African team Hellenic. The club, which won the Israeli Cup final last season, is based in Sakhnin, near Haifa. Renowned for their strong ethical principles, they are prominent advocates for peace and cooperation within Israel.

I initially purchased this game during its early development phase, back when the only currency available was spacebux and there was no "tech tree." This was when the game truly shone. Fellow TEDsters, we find ourselves navigating profoundly challenging economic waters—times that test resilience and demand innovative perspectives. Yet amidst this turbulence, I wish to share an extraordinary, if often overlooked, narrative of commercial triumph over the past two decades. This phenomenon stands as a singular parallel—in its own unconventional manner—to the meteoric rises of Microsoft or Google. Remarkably, this sector has weathered our current economic downturn with striking composure.  

I speak, of course, of organized crime.  

Now, you might rightly observe that syndicated criminal enterprises are hardly a recent development—and you’d be absolutely correct. But the past twenty years have witnessed an unparalleled surge in their scale and influence, with illicit networks now contributing an estimated 15% of global GDP. I’ve come to term this sprawling underworld the "Global Shadow Economy"—or, more succinctly, *McMafia*.  

So what catalyzed this staggering expansion of transnational crime?

The story in Fallout 3 was superior. Settlements interfere with the narrative to such an extent that you'll find managing them frustrating instead of focusing on the story. Some keys are hard-coded and can’t be reassigned. The game is rife with bugs. At one point, my Pip-Boy disappeared permanently, and I had to research console commands to retrieve it. Power armor is practically handed to you from the start, and power cores are plentiful in trader shops. This significantly diminishes the value of power armor compared to previous Fallout games. The story is mediocre, and the game overall is average—though it doesn’t crash every 10 minutes like Fallout 3, making it more stable by comparison. Is it better than Fallout 3? No. Better than New Vegas? No. This title was tremendously overhyped considering how disappointing it truly is.

We begin in 1964. Bob Dylan is 23 years old, and his career is just reaching its pinnacle. He's been christened the voice of a generation and is churning out classic songs at a seemingly impossible rate. However, there's a small minority of dissenters who claim that Bob Dylan is stealing other people's songs. Fast forward to 2004, Brian Burton, aka Danger Mouse, takes The Beatles' "White Album" and combines it with Jay-Z's "The Black Album" to create "The Grey Album." "The Grey Album" quickly becomes an online sensation, prompting The Beatles' record company to send out countless cease-and-desist letters for "unfair competition and dilution of our valuable property." Now, "The Grey Album" is regarded as a remix.

Welcome, TED community, to our live conversation today. I'm Chris Anderson, and I'm excited to be here with you all. Today's discussion is a crucial one, as it addresses the pressing issues that have arisen in the wake of the pandemic and the recent tragic events surrounding the death of George Floyd and the ongoing protests. We had initially conceptualized the 'build back better' series to tackle the challenges posed by the pandemic, but the past week's events have added a new layer of urgency to our conversation. Before we can even begin to think about how we can build back better, we must first grapple with the enormity of this moment. We must seek to understand the depth of the pain, the fear, and the resilience of those affected. Whitney Pennington Rodgers  That's right, Chris.

Advances in spintronic nanodevices have led to the development of ultrafast nonlinear dynamic and recurrence behaviors on a nanosecond scale, paving the way for the creation of spintronic reservoir computing (RC) systems. This study proposes and models two novel physical RC systems, one based on a single magnetic skyrmion memristor (MSM) and the other on an array of 24 spin-torque nano-oscillators (STNOs), designed to tackle image classification tasks and nonlinear dynamic system prediction, respectively. Micromagnetic simulation results reveal that the nonlinear responses of MSM and STNO to current pulse stimulation hold great promise for RC applications. Notably, a single MSM-based RC system demonstrates exceptional performance in handwritten digit recognition, outperforming existing solutions. Furthermore, a 24-STNO-based RC system is shown to effectively solve complex, unknown nonlinear dynamic problems, as evidenced by its successful application to a second-order nonlinear dynamic system and the NARMA10 task, thereby highlighting the potential of spintronic RC systems in addressing challenging computational tasks.

I have always prided myself on making anyone who frequents Casa Pescatore as comfortable as possible. I want them to feel as at home as they would in their own residences. Yesterday, I hope I succeeded. The masses congregated on Perry Ave with what many consider 'extreme prejudice.' At 4:30 PM, my best friend Eric and his son Nunu came over with a huge pan of baked ziti. The tables were set, and I had all the food I cooked ready, and the bells were ringing like crazy. Before I could blink, my best friend from Florida walked through the door, and that made me smile. I missed that guy so much. The bonus was him bringing his wife along. She is expecting, and this is the closest I could get to being an uncle. I spoiled her with tray after tray of food and tiramisu.

Menstruation. A simple word describing a natural biological process, weighed down by centuries of stigma, has been transformed into something most of us can only speak about in whispers. But why? As I speak to you right now, more than 800 million women around the world are having a period. None of us would exist without it, and yet it remains an "embarrassing" subject to broach. From my experience and that of the women around me, I can tell you that it's exhausting. It's exhausting to carefully take out a brown paper bag hiding a pad, stuff it into your pocket in the middle of a class, and rush to the washroom as discreetly as possible. It's exhausting to sit through lessons and meetings pretending to be absolutely normal, while internally crying out from intense period cramps.

I know in a previous post, I protected parents, almost to the point where I even took their side. Here's my change of heart. Parents don't know anything. They think they know you so well, maybe even to the point where they can read emotions off your face. They can't. Parents know nothing about us. And when I go upstairs after arguing with Nicole about Kelli, like I just did a few minutes ago, and they want me to tell them what's wrong. Yeah, how about you shut the fuck up!? I don't have to tell you what's wrong all the time. Here's the situation with Nicole and Kelli. Kelli and Nicole don't get along. They don't like each other at all.

Metal ion implantation into ceramics has been demonstrated to be an effective and controllable technique for tailoring the surface electrical conductivity of the ceramic piece, and this approach has been used in numerous applications. Importantly, it provides a method for grading the voltage drop across high-voltage insulators, thereby increasing the maximum operational voltage that can be applied across the insulator without surface flashover. However, a concern for the use of this method is the long-term stability of the implantation-induced conductivity, especially if the implanted metal species is readily oxidized. Here we report on our examination of the long-term behavior of the surface conductivity of titanium-implanted alumina. The results indicate that after an initial drop of as much as 40% in the first few weeks after implantation, the conductivity shows only a very slow decrease of about 10% over the following year.

The traditional home phone is on the cusp of becoming an obsolete relic, as an increasing number of individuals rely solely on their mobile devices for all their calling needs. A comprehensive study conducted by Nokia reveals that over 45 million people in the UK, Germany, the US, and South Korea have abandoned their landlines in favor of mobile phones. Although the primary reason for retaining a fixed line phone is the lower call charges, the majority of respondents unequivocally believe that the future of telecommunications lies in mobile technology. The research, sponsored by Nokia, highlights a distinct dichotomy in the usage patterns of mobile and fixed phones. While home phones are predominantly used for extended conversations, mobile phones are favored for shorter, more casual exchanges, particularly among friends. Notably, in the UK, a staggering 69% of respondents cited cost as the primary reason for opting for their fixed phone, underscoring the lingering appeal of traditional landlines in an increasingly mobile-centric world.

Cabinet anger at Brown cash raid: Ministers are unhappy about plans to use Whitehall cash to keep council tax bills down, Local Government Minister Nick Raynsford has acknowledged. Gordon Brown reallocated £512m from central to local government budgets in his pre-budget report on Thursday. Mr. Raynsford said he had held some "pretty frank discussions" with fellow ministers over the plans. However, he mentioned that local governments had to deliver good services without significant council tax rises. The central government cash is part of a £1bn package to help local authorities in England keep next year's council tax increases below 5% in what is likely to be a general election year. Mr. Raynsford stated that nearly all central government departments had an interest in well-run local authorities.

Rosneft, a Russian state-owned oil company, has been ordered by a US court to pay $27 billion (£14 billion) in damages to Yukos, a US-based oil company, for violating US bankruptcy law. Yukos had filed for bankruptcy in the US in an attempt to halt the forced sale of its key oil production unit, Yuganskneftegas, which was sold to Rosneft for $9.4 billion in a state-led auction. However, the sale was disrupted due to legal challenges, and Yukos is now seeking damages from Rosneft for the harm caused by the auction.
According to Yukos' lawyers, the auction violated US bankruptcy law, and the company and its main shareholders have vowed to go after any company that buys its assets, using all and every legal means available. The dispute is ongoing, with Yukos seeking to have the sale of Yuganskneftegas set aside and the assets returned to the company.
