Sid Meier's Civilization: Beyond Earth is a turn-based strategy game where your objective is to develop your civilization to achieve specific goals or 'victories'. Unlike the previous installments, which unfolded against Earth's historical timeline—covering eras like the Stone Age, Medieval Age, and the Modern Age—this game is set in a futuristic scenario where your mission is to colonize a new planet. What I particularly appreciate about this game is that 2K Games took a fresh approach by implementing innovative mechanics such as the affinity system and the orbital layer. However, I found that these new mechanics could sometimes make the game feel a bit overwhelming. An area where I believe the game could be enhanced is by incorporating new artwork, particularly in the environments you encounter during interactions with other leaders.

Understanding the evolution of graphs over time is a complex task across fields like bioinformatics, physics, and social networks. In the scenario we examine, graph edges represent either short-term interactions (such as messaging) or long-term structural connections (such as friendship). Often, long-term edges are manually defined by humans, which can be costly to establish and may not always be optimal for subsequent analysis. To address these challenges, we introduce a model that combines temporal point processes with variational autoencoders to understand temporal attention between nodes based on their communication patterns. By using the dynamics of node interactions, this method enhances flexibility and resolves issues related to manually defined edges, as temporal attention influences how features are propagated between nodes.

I apologize for not being able to reveal my face, as doing so would put me in danger from those who mean harm. My journey began 14 years ago when I was a young reporter fresh out of college. I stumbled upon a scoop, a seemingly straightforward story about police officers accepting bribes from street hawkers. As a novice journalist, I wanted to approach it creatively to ensure it had maximum impact, especially since this corruption was an open secret yet continued unchecked. I decided to go undercover and pose as a vendor, allowing me to capture indisputable evidence of the wrongdoing. The results were outstanding and had a significant impact. This approach is often referred to as immersion journalism or undercover journalism.

I would like to tell you about the most embarrassing thing that has ever happened to me during my years of working as a palliative care physician. This happened a couple of years ago. I was asked as a consultant to see a woman in her 70s—a retired English professor with pancreatic cancer. I was asked to see her because she had pain, nausea, and vomiting. When I went to see her, we talked about those symptoms, and during that consultation, she asked me whether I thought that medical marijuana might help her. I thought back to everything I had learned in medical school about medical marijuana, which didn't take very long because I had learned absolutely nothing.

It's the gadgets you upgrade and acquire during the campaign that add nice new gameplay mechanics. Gaining access to previously unreachable areas creates a good sense of progression.

Where does the carbon dioxide we release go? Carbon plays a crucial role in a natural cycle, present in all living organisms  in the soil, in the oceans, in the atmosphere, and in rocks deep beneath the Earth's surface. When plants and other living organisms die, a significant portion of their stored carbon is released back to the atmosphere, where it is absorbed by new plants and microorganisms, perpetuating the cycle. However, over millions of years, some of the carbon stored in ancient trees, sea life, and other organisms becomes trapped in rock formations, eventually transforming into fossil fuels  coal, oil, and natural gas. Natural geological events, such as volcanic eruptions, release some of this trapped carbon, while human activities, like the burning of fossil fuels, also release a significant amount of prehistoric carbon dioxide. In fact, human activities today release approximately 60 times more carbon dioxide than all the volcanic eruptions on the planet each year, highlighting the profound impact of human activities on the global carbon cycle.

Seven years ago, I graduated from Berkeley with dual degrees in molecular and cell biology and linguistics. At a career fair on campus, I secured an interview with a startup called Theranos. Back then, there wasn’t much publicly available information about the company, but what little existed was genuinely impressive. Essentially, Theranos was developing a medical device capable of running a full blood panel using just a finger-stick sample. This innovation was compelling not only because it minimized the discomfort of traditional blood draws but also because it held the potential to revolutionize predictive diagnostics.

In this paper, we investigate the landscape of the population negative log-likelihood function of Gaussian mixture models with a general number of components. Due to non-convexity, multiple local minima exist that are not globally optimal, even when the mixture is well-separated. We demonstrate that all local minima share the same structural form that partially identifies the component centers of the true mixture, in the sense that each local minimum involves a non-overlapping combination of fitting multiple Gaussians to a single true component and fitting a single Gaussian to multiple true components. Our results apply to the setting where the true mixture components satisfy a certain separation condition, and are valid even when the number of components is over- or under-specified. For Gaussian mixtures with three components, we obtain sharper results in terms of the scaling with the separation between the components.
